Checking allows Elon to continue bluffing with missed draws (like 79s or 75s) or bet for value with hands I beat (like TX or overpairs). If I lead out, I might fold out his bluffs and only get called by hands that have me beat, such as a better six, a straight, or a full house. I intend to check-call most standard bet sizes to reach showdown.

River 3d is a complete blank: doesn't complete straights (no 79, 457), flushes (spades/diamonds/hearts draws miss), and pairs the deuces weakly. Flash's line—calling pre from BB (wide range: suited connectors, Ax, pairs), calling flop c-bet (misses most but floats/draws/gutshots like 97, J9, A9, 77-99), calling large turn bet after check (now boards pair 6s, improving some like 66/T6 but he passes on aggression), and now checking river—screams medium strength with showdown value: likely weak pairs (88/T8/66? unlikely pre-call, TT/JT/99/77), stubborn floats that bricked (A9/J9), or bottom two-pair/bottom set afraid of higher. Unlikely premium monsters (sets, full houses) given passive check-check-check line; he'd bomb turn/river. Our Ac Qh: pure ace-high bluff with premium blockers (A blocks AT/A6/A8/top pair Ax; Q blocks QJ/QT for TP). We've rep'd exactly 66/T6/86/T8/97/A6 range whole way via aggression; position + history of Flash folding rivers/checks to bets (Hands 10,11,24) = high fold equity. Pot 3350, bet ~60% (2000) sizes to ~5350, poor odds for his calls without nuts/draws. Stack commitment low (6930->4930), if raised easy fold (behind range), if called likely beat but realize via FE. Checking concedes pot, gifts free showdown, kills bluff equity. Aggro line maximizes EV deepstacked.

I have three-of-a-kind (sixes) with an Eight and a Ten as my high kickers (best hand: 6-6-6-T-8). The river card 3d is a brick and doesn't change the board texture significantly. Elon has bet 2000 into a 3350 pot, which is approximately 60% of the pot. Given Elon's position on the button and his history of aggression, his range includes overpairs (JJ-AA), top pair (TX), missed straight draws (like 75s or 97s), and potentially better sixes (A6s, K6s). While I am vulnerable to a better kicker or a full house (TT, 88), folding trips in this spot against a wide button opening range would be too exploitable. Raising would only isolate me against hands that beat me. Calling allows me to reach showdown and win against his bluffs and thinner value bets like overpairs or top pairs.

4-handed game makes positions looser. 76d is a suited connector with good playability, straight and flush potential. UTG open-raise is standard here to build pot or take it down. Raising to 2.5x BB (250 total) sizes appropriately given my 49BB stack and opponents' deeper stacks. Avoid limping as it invites multiway pots without initiative. Folding is too tight for short-handed play.

Calling with AT on the button in position. The hand is well above average, and button position gives me a significant post-flop advantage. Elon's standard UTG raise doesn't indicate extreme strength. With favorable pot odds and the ability to play well post-flop, this is a profitable call. I can easily fold if I miss or face aggression, and I can maximize value if I hit.

I am in the Small Blind with Ten-Four offsuit (Ts 4c). Elon raised from UTG and Claude called on the Button. This is a very weak hand to play out of position against a pre-flop raiser and a caller. Folding is the most prudent play to conserve my stack for better opportunities.

Facing an UTG open to 250 with the button cold-calling, I’m in the big blind and out of position in a multiway pot. J♥4♠ is a weak, often-dominated hand with very poor postflop playability here. Calling 150 into a 650 pot requires ~18.8% equity, and versus an UTG opening range plus a button caller my equity and playability are both marginal. Three-betting as a bluff OOP into two players is too thin and will create difficult spots. Fold and preserve my stack for a better spot in position.

Preflop raise with 76d suited connector was standard UTG in short-handed play for its playability and potential. Flop Th 2d Td gives me a strong nut flush draw (9 outs) plus backdoor straight potential, solid equity (~35-40% vs typical calling range). Paired board reduces straight draws but increases chance opponent has a ten. As preflop raiser acting first heads-up, c-bet semi-bluff applies fold equity against Claude's button calling range (which includes Ax, broadways, suited connectors, some pocket pairs below tens). 60% pot bet sizes well for value from worse draws/pairs while protecting my draw and building pot for when flush hits. Checking risks giving free card to overcards or draws; betting maintains initiative per my aggressive image from history (e.g., Hand 5, 24, 36). Stack depth supports this (47BB).

Raising with trip tens (Ah Tc on Th 2d Td board). This is a very strong hand on a dry flop. Elon's 400 bet from UTG could range from strong hands to continuation bets. With my hand strength and his relatively short stack (4280), I should raise to build the pot and extract value. A raise to 1000 puts him in a decision spot while keeping him interested in the hand. This balances value extraction with pot building on a premium holding.

Nut diamond flush draw (9 clean outs to nuts, ~35-40% equity vs Claude's flop raise range: trips Tx, sets TT/22, overpairs QQ+, weaker FDs, combo draws, bluffs/air). Pot odds for call superb (600 into 2650 pot ~18-22% equity needed, we crush). However, shoving ~42BB remaining maximizes overall EV: high fold equity vs light button raises (Claude history: folds to postflop aggression in H1, H5 indirectly, H17, H29; calls big but not always deep like H21). Extracts value from dominated Tx/bad kicker, worse draws (e.g., 98dd, A3dd), overpairs/pairs fearing sets/Tx. Protects by charging draws/overcards. Builds massive pot (~11k) for flush. Consistent UTG aggro image (won H24 top pair cbet, H36 AQ value bet streets). Deep opponent covered but post-shove SPR ~1, realizes equity fully. Call passive (free turn card), fold nitty (-EV). Jam optimal pro play short-handed.

Calling with trip tens (Ah Tc on Th 2d Td board). This is an extremely strong hand with an ace kicker. Elon's all-in shove after my raise indicates a committed hand, but his range includes many hands I beat (lower pairs, whiffed broadway cards). I need only 37% equity to break even, and I estimate 58-60% equity against his plausible range. The only hands that significantly beat me are TT (quads) and 22/2T (full house), which represent a minority of his likely holdings. The pot odds strongly favor a call here.

I am on the button with Td 7c. In a 3-handed game, this is a hand I can occasionally raise with to take the blinds, but with a stack of 13975 and a pot of only 150, it is a marginal hand. However, the table dynamics show significant aggression from Minni and Claude. Claude just won a massive pot in Hand #37 and has the chip lead. T7o is generally a fold from the button in a standard strategy unless I intend to steal, but against these opponents who have been active, folding is the safest way to maintain my stack for better spots.

I’m in the small blind with A♥9♣ heads-up vs the big blind. This is a hand I want to play for initiative rather than just completing and being out-of-position postflop. A9o has an Ace-blocker and decent high-card value heads-up, so I’ll raise to isolate Claude and get fold equity. I’ll make the standard min/2x raise to 200 (keeps the pot manageable vs a deep-stacked, often-active opponent). If I face a large 3-bet I’ll re-evaluate (likely fold), but if called I’ll continue with a c-bet plan on favorable flops and pot-control on wet boards.

Folding Js6c from the BB against Minni's SB raise. While I'm getting 4:1 odds, this hand plays poorly out of position and I have a strong stack with ample opportunities ahead. No need to take marginal spots when stronger hands will develop.

On the button with Th-2s — essentially junk. The pot is 150 and it costs me 100 to see the flop, so I’d need ~40% equity to justify a call. Versus the small- and big-blind defending/3-betting ranges (both players are deep and have shown aggression), T2o is heavily dominated and has far less equity than that. A raise to 200 as a blind-steal is possible in some games, but here Claude and Flash have shown frequent 3-bets/plays for stacks — raising with this hand just invites trouble. I’ll preserve my stack and fold this weak holding preflop.

Folding 9d 4h from the small blind in a heads-up situation against Flash. This is one of the weakest hands in poker - 9-4 offsuit has very poor equity and no practical value. While it would only cost 50 more chips to call, there's no strategic benefit to entering the pot with such a poor holding. Even though we've played aggressively in previous hands, hand selection is critical for long-term profitability. Better to preserve chips and wait for stronger holdings where we have better equity and decision-making options post-flop.

Holding Ad6d on the button in a 3-handed game against Flash (SB) and Minni (BB). While Ad6d is a weak hand in isolation, several factors make calling justified here: (1) I have premium position as the button - I'll act last preflop and first postflop, (2) the pot odds are favorable (150 to win risking 100), (3) I'm facing only two opponents with reasonable-sized blinds to potentially win, (4) an ace plays well in position even with a weak kicker, and (5) my stack is healthy at 17,980. The hand has showdown value and I can leverage position to control the pot postflop. Folding here would be too conservative given the positional advantage and favorable circumstances.

I am in the Small Blind with Queen-Jack offsuit, which is a solid hand in short-handed play. The Button (Claude) has limped into the pot. To gain initiative and potentially win the pot immediately or isolate the limper, I will put in a raise. A raise to 400 (4x the big blind) is a standard size to discourage the Big Blind from defending lightly and to put pressure on Claude's limping range.

Holding 4♣2♠ (off-suit) I'm facing a raise to 400 from the small blind after the button limped. Pot is 600 and I must call 300. This is a multiway, out-of-position spot against an aggressor and a caller; 4-2o has virtually no showdown or postflop playability and will be dominated by the ranges that would limp/call and raise here. Calling 300 for marginal pot odds (~3:1) doesn’t justify playing a hand with almost no equity or fold equity, and a raise to isolate would be pure spew. Preserve my stack for better spots — fold.

Analyzing Hand #40: I'm on the button with Ad6d facing Flash's SB raise to 400 after I called 100 preflop. Minni folded from the BB, so this is now heads-up.

Key factors for my decision:
1. **Position advantage**: I'm on the button and will act last postflop - this is substantial leverage in position.
2. **Pot odds**: I'm getting 3:1 (risking 300 to win 600), which is reasonable for a marginal hand.
3. **Hand potential**: Ad6d has some merit:
   - Ace-high with showdown value
   - Two diamonds provides flush draw potential
   - Can catch favorable flops (ace, pair, diamonds)
4. **Opponent assessment**: Flash is aggressive (evidenced by many SB raises), but Minni's BB fold suggests this isn't a huge hand on Flash's end.
5. **Stack situation**: I have 17,880 remaining with a healthy stack, so I'm not in desperation mode.

**Decision: CALL**

In position with reasonable odds and good hand playability potential, calling allows me to:
- Control the pot postflop from position
- Exploit the positional advantage to apply pressure
- Make an informed decision based on board texture
- Play small ball with a hand that can improve or catch favorable situations

A fold would be too conservative given the odds and positional equity. A raise would be too thin with Ad6d given that Flash's raise already indicates some strength.

I have flopped middle pair (Jacks) with an open-ended straight draw (any Ace or Nine completes the straight) on a very coordinated board of Kh-Tc-Jd. While I was the pre-flop aggressor, this specific board texture hits a button-limping and calling range quite hard, as it includes many Broadway combinations. Checking is a prudent move here to control the pot size and see how Claude reacts. With my current hand strength and significant draw equity, I am comfortable calling a bet, but checking allows me the possibility of seeing a free turn card if Claude checks back.

I'm first to act on a coordinated flop (Kh Tc Jd) with Ad6d in position against Flash who just checked. Flash's check indicates potential weakness after raising preflop, creating an opportunity. With Ad6d, I have a 9-card diamond flush draw (plus 3 aces = ~12 total outs), giving me approximately 35-40% equity against most hands in Flash's preflop raising range (AK, AQ, QQ, lower pairs). Betting 500 (about 55% pot) accomplishes several goals: (1) takes down the pot immediately against many hands, (2) builds the pot with my strong equity from the flush draw, (3) leverages my positional advantage and opponent's checked weakness, (4) keeps the bet size manageable with my marginal current hand strength. Checking would be too passive and concede free equity on a turn card. A larger bet risks running into strong holdings unnecessarily. The flush draw gives me sufficient equity to justify aggression here.

I have flopped middle pair (Jacks) with an open-ended straight draw on a very connected board. Claude bet 500 into a 900 pot (making it 1400 total). I need to call 500 to win a total pot of 1900, giving me roughly 3.8:1 odds. Given my current hand strength and the fact that I have 8 outs to a straight plus potential outs to improve my pair, I have more than enough equity to justify a call. Raising is risky because the board hits many hands in a limp-calling range (like KT, KJ, or AQ), so calling allows me to see the turn and realize my equity without over-inflating the pot out of position.

The Turn card is the Jack of Clubs, which is an excellent card for me as it gives me trips (three of a kind). The board texture is still very coordinated (Kh-Tc-Jd-Jc), meaning straights like AQ or full houses like TT and KJ are possible given Claude's button-limp and flop-betting line. However, my hand is now significantly stronger. By checking here, I can maintain pot control and encourage Claude to continue bluffing or betting with a King or a Ten, which I now beat. If Claude checks behind, I can evaluate the River. If he bets, I will most likely call to realize my equity and keep his range wide rather than raising and potentially isolating myself against the very top of his range.

Analyzing the turn: I hold Ad6d on a board of Kh Tc Jd Jc. Flash has checked twice (flop and turn), suggesting either a missed hand (AK, AQ) or potential weakness. My current hand strength is just ace-high with limited outs - only 3 aces and 3 sixes (6 clean outs, approximately 13% equity on the river). 

The paired Jack on turn is a significant card that likely didn't help Flash's preflop raise range unless he holds TT for a set. Given my weak equity and that Flash has shown checking behavior, betting here has poor risk-reward: (1) better hands in his range will call (TT, QQ, KK, AA), (2) my equity doesn't justify a thin value bet or bluff, (3) checking allows me to see the river for free and makes a decision based on whether I improve.

The correct line is to CHECK and take the free river card. If I miss on the river, I can fold to aggression, and if I hit an ace or six, I'll have a made hand to potentially win with. This preserves chips with a weak hand and allows the board to run out while maintaining optionality on the river.

I have improved to Three of a Kind (Jacks) with a Queen kicker on a board that is quite coordinated. After I checked the turn and Claude checked back, it's possible he has a hand with showdown value like a King or a Ten, or a missed straight draw. By betting now on the river, I can extract value from a King or potentially a Ten that might call a mid-sized bet. While AQ (a straight) or full houses (KJ, TT) are possible, Claude's check on the turn makes those hands slightly less likely or suggests he was playing them cautiously. I will bet 950 (half the pot) to get called by worse hands while keeping the pot manageable if he has me beat.

**River Analysis - Hand #40**

Current situation: I'm on the river with Ad6d facing Flash's 950 raise. Board: Kh Tc Jd Jc 5c

**Flash's betting pattern assessment:**
- Preflop: Raised to 400 (indicates opening range, likely AK, AQ, pocket pairs, strong broadway cards)
- Flop: Checked after my 500 bet (showed weakness or was giving up on a marginal holding)
- Turn: Checked again after the 5c came (further suggested his hand either missed or he was trapping)
- River: Now raising to 950 after checking twice

**The critical tell:** A river raise after TWO checks strongly suggests Flash either:
1. Hit something on the river (unlikely given board run-out - Kh Tc Jd Jc 5c)
2. Is value betting a made hand from the preflop (KK, QQ, TT for sets; KJ, QJ for two pair)
3. Is semi-bluffing with a hand that's ahead of mine

**My hand strength:** Ad6d = just ace-high with no made value. Against Flash's likely holdings (sets, two pair, strong broadway), my equity is minimal.

**Pot odds calculation:** 
- Pot is 2850, need to call 950
- Getting 3:1 odds (need 25% equity to break even)
- My actual equity: ~15-20% at best
  - Against TT/JJ: ~10-15%
  - Against two pair (KJ, QJ): ~15-20%
  - Unlikely against AK/AQ as those would have raised earlier

**Stack consideration:** I have 17080 remaining - plenty of chips, no pressure to gamble.

**Conclusion:** This is a clear FOLD. Flash's river aggression after checking through two streets indicates a constructed hand with strong equity. My ace-high has insufficient showdown value and I don't have the equity required to justify calling at these odds. Folding preserves chips for better opportunities.

I am on the button with 8c 3c. While being on the button in a 3-handed game allows for a wide opening range, 8-3 suited is near the bottom of the range and doesn't provide enough playability to justify a raise against active opponents like Minni and Claude. I will fold and wait for a better spot.

I’m in the small blind with 2♣3♥ — a very weak, non‑suited, low connector with almost no playability out of position. Pot is 150 and it costs only 50 to call, but with deep effective stacks and Claude (BB) showing a tendency to defend and get aggressive (many prior hands where he calls/raises), raising as a steal is unlikely to succeed and will often get me into difficult OOP postflop spots. Calling to see a flop looks cheap but commits me to play OOP with a hand that will rarely realize its equity and will fold to standard continuation bets. Long‑term EV is maximized by preserving chips for better spots, so I fold my SB and avoid marginal OOP play.

I'm on the button with a small pocket pair (4h4c), stacks ~78bb. Facing only the blinds, I want to take the initiative, narrow the field and retain set-mining value in position. A standard open to ~3x the BB (raise to 300) gives fold equity vs the blinds, reduces multiway risk compared to just calling 100, and keeps the pot manageable if I get called. If I face a significant 3-bet I'll fold and wait for a better spot; if called I'll play postflop in position looking to hit a set or use position to take the pot.

2-5 suited is an extremely weak hand, even with both cards being spades. In the small blind facing Minni's raise to 300, this hand does not warrant further investment for several reasons: (1) The hand has minimal equity against Minni's raising range from the button; (2) I would be out of position for the entire hand; (3) Even with pot odds of 1.8:1, this hand cannot justify calling against aggressive opponents; (4) Minni has been raising frequently and there's substantial risk of additional aggression from Flash in the big blind. This is a clear fold - I should preserve my stack and wait for stronger opportunities.

I am in the Big Blind with King of Clubs and Trey of Hearts (K3o). Minni has raised to 3x (300) from the Button. While a King-high hand has some equity against a wide button opening range in a 3-handed game, K3o has poor playability post-flop and is frequently dominated by better Kings (K4s+, K8o+). From the button with As6d, I'm facing a small blind (Flash: 14,875) and big blind (Minni: 7,895). To call costs only 100 chips against a 150-chip pot, giving excellent pot odds.

Key considerations:
1. **Position**: Button is the most advantageous position - I act last post-flop with maximum information
2. **Hand strength**: As6d is marginal, but the ace is the best starting card in poker. It has playability
3. **Stack depths**: I have 17,080 chips (healthy), Minni is short-stacked at 7,895
4. **Cost-benefit**: 100 chips is tiny relative to my stack (0.6% of stack)
5. **Opponent tendencies**: From previous hands, Minni is aggressive but with 7,895 remaining, I have fold equity if needed

With position advantage, I can control the hand post-flop. If I miss the flop, I can check back and reassess. If an ace hits or I find a favorable flop, I can apply pressure from position.

A raise would overplay this marginal hand - better to use positional advantage to play post-flop poker where I can outplay weaker opponents with my stack and position.

I am in the Small Blind with Jack-Eight offsuit. Claude has limped from the Button. While the pot odds to call are attractive (50 to win a total pot of 250, or 5:1), J8o is a weak hand that plays poorly out of position post-flop, especially in a potential multi-way pot. Minni in the Big Blind has been active and could easily raise, forcing a fold and wasting the 50 chips. I will choose to fold and wait for a better opportunity.

Td9o (10♦9♠) is a connected but offsuit, marginal hand and I’m out of position against a button limp. Claude’s limp looks like a wide, often passive range, but raising here mostly just bloats a pot I’ll be first to act in — not ideal with an offsuit connector. I’ll check to keep the pot under control and realize equity cheaply. Postflop plan: if Claude checks I’ll see the flop free; if he bets small (~<=1/3–1/2 pot) I’ll call with pair or good draws and fold to heavy pressure if I miss; if I hit top pair/strong draw I’ll play aggressively. I’ll only use a preflop raise here with stronger holdings (pocket pairs, broadway, or suited connectors).

I hold Td9s on Qc Kh Tc — a single pair of tens with a 9-kicker. That’s a marginal, vulnerable made hand: Claude’s limp range is wide but includes lots of Kx/Qx and straight-makers (e.g. J9, AJ). I’m out of position and there’s no bet to face, so pot control and cheap showdown are preferable to bloating the pot. I’ll check to see his action: call small bets with this pair to realize equity, fold to large river/turn pressure if I don’t improve, and only apply aggression if I pick up two-pair/strong draws or a favorable read. Betting here would risk getting called or raised by better K/Q/straight hands, so checking is the lower-variance + EV-preserving play.

As6d on Qc Kh Tc is ace-high with weak kicker on a coordinated broadway board. Minni checked to me from the BB. I should check and see another card rather than bet a marginal hand into this dangerous flop texture. Position and pot control are more valuable here than trying to win the pot immediately with a hand that's likely not the best.

I have T9o on Qc Kh Tc 6c — a single pair of tens with a weak 9 kicker. This is a vulnerable middle pair: there are overcards (K,Q) on board and three clubs out there, so flushes and higher pairs are plausible. Claude has shown passive lines (limp, check, check), so I’m not getting many folds and could be crushed if I bloat the pot out of position. Pot control is the priority: I’ll check the turn to see the river for free. Plan: if I improve (two pair/trips) or get a safe runout I’ll value bet; if I miss the river I’ll call small-to-medium bets to realize equity but fold to large pressure given the clothed board texture.

On the turn with As6d against a coordinated Qc Kh Tc 6c board, I have bottom pair - a weak hand in this texture. Betting is risky; I could easily be beat by broadway pairs or draws that complete on the river. Minni's check-check-check line from BB suggests weakness. I check to: (1) see the river in position, (2) maintain pot control with a marginal hand, (3) let weaker hands potentially bluff the river, and (4) avoid committing chips with a hand that's likely behind strong opponent ranges. Position advantage allows me to make a better river decision with complete information.

I check. The river 2c turns the board Qc Kh Tc 6c 2c into a very club-heavy texture (four clubs), so any single club in Claude's hand now makes a flush. I only have one pair (tens) with a weak 9-kicker and I'm out of position; Claude's line (limp, check, check) is passive but wide, and the river increases the number of hands that beat me. There's little fold equity and a value bet won't get called by many worse hands while risking a raise from a flush or K/Q. Best low-variance play is to check to showdown and call small river bets to realize equity, folding to large bets/raises.

On the river with As6d against a paired board (6c visible), I hold a weak pair of sixes. The board is dangerously coordinated (Qc Kh Tc 6c 2c) with four clubs visible. Minni has checked all three streets (flop, turn, river), indicating a weak holding throughout. Betting with this marginal hand risks chips with little upside - I'd only win the 250-chip pot if she folds, but risk significant chips if she calls and I'm beaten by broadway cards or higher pairs. My pair of sixes likely has showdown value given her weakness. Checking takes the hand to showdown and preserves chips while letting her weak range check down. This is pot control with a marginal hand on a scary board.

I am on the button with T3o. This is a very weak hand. While I could attempt a steal, the players in the blinds (Minni and Claude) have shown significant aggression in previous hands. T3o does not play well post-flop, and I have no reason to invest chips here with such a subpar starting hand.

Heads-up in the small blind with Tc5s — a weak, marginal hand but cheap to see a flop. Pot is 150 and I only have to call 50, so the price is good (roughly 20% equity required). Claude is deep-stacked and has shown willingness to play back and raise; min-raising from the SB with T5o likely gets 3-bet or bloats a pot I don’t want OOP. I’ll call the 50 to see a flop cheaply, keep the pot under control, and use my positional advantage postflop to make decisions. Postflop plan: play straightforward — value-bet tens, call small/medium c-bets with some backdoor equity, fold to large, committed pressure on dry boards.

6-5 of hearts is a weak hand but worth seeing a flop when I can do so for free. Minni's weak small blind call suggests a marginal hand, and with only two players remaining post-flop, I'll have positional advantage. Checking allows me to evaluate my hand's potential while keeping my range balanced - I can check strong hands too. If I connect with a pair, flush draw, or straight draw, I can continue; if I miss, I can fold to aggression. This is standard big blind defense with a speculative hand facing weak opposition.

I have top pair (Tens) with a weak 5-kicker on Kc Th Qc. Heads-up this is a hand I want to extract value from — worse tens, Qx and many Jx/AJ-type hands will pay a bet, and I also block a chunk of club combos by holding Tc. The board has dangerous broadway/street possibilities, so betting protects my hand and denies free cards. Pot is 200 so a ~50% pot bet (100) balances value and pot control; if Claude calls I can re-evaluate the turn, if he raises big he most likely has K/Q/straight and I can fold or proceed cautiously. Bet 100 to get value and define his range.

Folding is the correct play here. I'm holding 6h 5h on a K-Q-T flop - a very weak hand with minimal equity. While I technically have pot odds (3:1, needing 25% equity), my actual equity is marginal at best:

- No made hand or strong draw: The broadway flop (K-Q-T) connects well for most continuing hands in Minni's range
- Weak flush draw: Need 2 more hearts in 2 cards, which is low probability
- No straight draw: 6-5 doesn't connect to K-Q-T in any meaningful way
- Out of position: If I call and miss the turn (which is likely), I'll face another bet and be forced to make a difficult decision

Minni's raise into me on a broadway flop suggests a strong hand or strong draw (likely has a piece of this board). My expected equity is likely 15-25% against her range, which is below break-even despite the pot odds. Given stack depths and position, folding preserves chips for better spots.

On the button with 4♦7♣: a weak, unsuited hand but I'm heads‑up against the blinds in a 3‑handed pot and have position. Opening wide from the button is standard — it steals blinds and lets me play post‑flop in position. I’ll raise to 300 (3x the BB) to apply pressure while keeping the pot controllable; if I face a sizeable 3‑bet I’ll fold rather than inflate a pot with a marginal holding. If called I’ll proceed cautiously post‑flop, looking to continue only with pair/strong draws or good equity and otherwise check‑control or fold to heavy aggression.

K8o is a marginal hand that plays poorly out of position. Facing a standard button raise from the aggressive Minni, this hand lacks the strength needed to call or raise from the small blind. The positional disadvantage post-flop combined with the weak hand makes folding the optimal play to preserve stack equity for better opportunities.

I am in the Big Blind with 9c6s. Minni, on the button, has raised to 300. This is a standard button steal attempt. However, 96 offsuit is a relatively weak hand to defend with out of position, even against a wide button range. Looking at previous hands, Minni has been quite active with pre-flop raises. Given my position and the weakness of my hand, folding is the most disciplined play to conserve my stack for better opportunities.

2-9o is one of the weakest possible starting hands. Even on the button with decent position, the hand's poor equity doesn't justify calling 100 chips. Pot odds of 1.5:1 are insufficient for such a marginal hand. Maintaining a disciplined approach of only playing strong holdings will maximize long-term profitability.

I am in the Small Blind with Jd 4c. This is a very weak hand, and although it only costs 50 more to call, it's generally better to fold these hands out of position, especially against Minni, who has been active in many pots. Folding is the safest and most disciplined play to avoid difficult post-flop situations with a marginal hand.

I have been dealt Pocket Kings (Ks Kd) on the button, the second-best starting hand in Texas Hold'em. With only 3 active players and being on the button, I have a massive equity advantage. I need to build a pot immediately. The standard play here is to raise. A 3x or 3.5x open is standard, so I will raise to 300 to induce action while extracting value from weaker hands that might defend the blinds.

Button opens to 300 (3BB) in a 3-handed pot. I have T♠8♦ in the small blind — a marginal, but defendable hand that wants to see a flop rather than get made into a 3-bet bluff OOP. A tiny 3-bet to 500 is weak and invites a shove or a larger 4-bet; a larger 3-bet would be overcommitting with poor position and mediocre equity. Calling 250 more (300 total committed) gives ~36% pot odds to see a flop against a wide opening range and keeps the pot manageable. Post-flop I’ll proceed cautiously: fold to heavy aggression on obvious A/K high boards, pot-control with second/weak pair or missed draws, and be ready to apply pressure if I pick up equity or see favorable action. Call to 300.

Folding T5o in a 3-way pot from the BB. While I'm getting 3.5:1 odds, my equity is marginal at ~20-25%, and I'm out of position post-flop. T5o is too weak to justify calling against two aggressive opponents when I have a healthy stack and can wait for better opportunities.

Check. I have T♠8♦ on Q♠9♣4♦ — I missed but hold an open-ended straight draw (7s or Js: 8 outs, ~31.5% to complete by the river). The board is dry and the preflop raiser (Flash) will c-bet a wide range; checking keeps the pot under control OOP, lets me induce a c-bet so I can realize my equity, and preserves the option to check‑raise as a semi-bluff if he bets small. If he fires a standard c-bet I plan to call and see the turn; if he checks back I’ll re-evaluate with the turn card.

I hold Pocket Kings on a board of Qs-9c-4d. This is a very safe board for my hand as I beat all single-pair hands except for trip Queens, trip Nines, or trip Fours, which are less likely given the pre-flop action. Minni checked, which indicates weakness or a potential trap with a medium-strength hand. I should bet here to extract value from any Queen-x, Nine-x, or straight draws (like J-10). A bet of about half the pot is standard to keep her range wide while still building the pot. I will bet 350.

Board Q♠ 9♣ 4♦, I hold T♠8♦ — I missed but have an open-ended straight draw (J or 7 = 8 outs, ~31.5% to hit by the river) plus a backdoor spade. Flash’s c-bet is small (350 into a 1050 pot, ~1/3 pot). Calling is +EV on pot odds (350 to win a 1400 pot → need ~25% equity and I have ~31.5%), but a raise is preferable here to leverage fold equity against a wide button c-bet range and to deny free cards. A min-raise to 700 applies pressure while keeping risk limited (only another 350 committed now, well under my 8145 stack). Plan: raise to 700 as a semi-bluff — if he folds I take the pot; if he calls I can realize my equity and proceed to the turn (bet big if I complete the straight or improve; exercise pot control or fold to heavy action if I miss and he shows strength). If he 3-bets large, I will re-evaluate (likely fold to an overbet without a made hand).

I hold Pocket Kings (Ks Kd) on a board of Qs-9c-4d. Minni has check-raised me from 350 to 700. This is a relatively small raise, often called a 'min-click,' which can sometimes represent a very strong hand like a set (99, 44) or a two-pair (Q9), but can also be a defensive move or a semi-bluff with a straight draw like JT. Given my hand strength and the fact that I have position, folding is out of the question. I could raise now, but if Minni has a set, I might face a large shove. By calling, I keep the pot controlled, hide the strength of my hand, and allow her to continue bluffing or betting into me on the turn with weaker hands like top pair. Calling is the most flexible option here to evaluate the turn while staying ahead of her most likely bluffs and one-pair hands.

I raised the flop as a semi‑bluff with an OESD (J or 7 = 8 outs). Turn A♣ (A♥ here) is a scary overcard that helps many hands in Flash’s calling range (Ax, Qx, 9x), but I still have 8 outs to complete the straight — 8/46 ≈ 17.4% to hit the river. Pot is 2,100 and I face no bet on the turn. A small turn bet (~¼ pot) is the best line: it (1) gets fold equity against parts of his calling range, and (2) if called gives me decent pot odds to chase — for example a 500 bet called makes the final pot ~3,100 and my called EV = 0.174*3,100 − 500 ≈ +39 chips, i.e. still +EV when called. Larger bets (≫560) make being called a losing proposition for my draw and invite hero‑folding to pressure. Checking surrenders fold equity and lets him take the initiative. Plan: fire a small semi‑bluff now. If he folds I take the pot; if he calls I realize equity and will only barrel the river if I improve (or face a very favorable runout/line); if he raises I fold — I won’t invest more with a one‑card draw OOP.

The Ace on the turn is a significant scare card because it fits well within Minni's range for calling a pre-flop raise from the Small Blind (hands like AQ, AJ, AT, or A9). However, her bet of 500 into a pot of approximately 2100 is very small (less than 25% of the pot). This small sizing often indicates a 'blocking bet' with a hand like a Queen that is now unsure where it stands, or a cautious bet with a marginal Ace. Given that I have Pocket Kings and am getting over 5-to-1 pot odds, folding is too conservative. Raising would likely only get called by hands that beat me (A-x, sets, or two pair). Therefore, calling is the most appropriate action to maintain pot control, utilize my position, and re-evaluate on the river.

River: Q♠ 9♣ 4♦ A♥ 4♣. I hold T♠8♦ — I completely missed my OESD and the river paired the board, creating full‑house / boat possibilities for any opponent holding a 4. I had been semi‑bluffing (raised flop, barreled turn) but now have essentially zero showdown value. Flash called both my flop raise and my turn bet, which strongly indicates a pair or better in his range (Qx, 9x, Ax, mid pocket pairs) and a relatively low fold frequency to a river bluff. Simple fold‑equity math: a 1,000 chip bluff into 3,100 needs ~24% folds to breakeven; a 500 chip bluff needs ~14% — given his line, I don’t trust he folds that often. With no real equity and high likelihood of being called, the prudent line is to check and conserve chips. If Flash bets, I’ll fold to any meaningful value bet; checking keeps the pot small and avoids throwing good chips away on a thin bluff.

On the river, the board is Qs-9c-4d-Ah-4c. I hold Pocket Kings, which was a powerhouse on the flop but became a marginal 'catch' hand once the Ace appeared on the turn. Minni's check on the river after her small turn bet suggests she either has a marginal hand like a Queen or a Nine and is looking for a cheap showdown, or she has an Ace and is checking to induce a bluff or play cautiously. Since it is unlikely she will call a bet with a hand worse than Kings (like a Queen) on an Ace-high board, there is no value in betting. Checking back allows me to realize my showdown value and win the pot against her missed draws or weaker pairs (Qx, JJ, TT) without risking a check-raise or being called by a better hand like an Ace.

I have premium pocket Aces on the button with ~69 BB effective and the pot is 150 (to call 100). Open-raising is virtually always correct here to build the pot, thin the field, and extract value from worse hands (broadways, pocket pairs). I’ll raise to 400 (4x the big blind) — big enough to charge speculative callers and isolate one opponent often, but not so large that I overcommit or fold out too many calling hands. If I face a reasonable 3-bet I’ll most often continue (call or 4-bet depending on sizing); versus an extreme shove I’ll evaluate but AA is usually ahead and will commit when pot odds/stack depth make it correct. Raising to 400 maximizes expected value while keeping postflop leverage.

Folding Q9o from the small blind. While Minni's button raise may be somewhat wide, Q9 offsuit is a marginal hand that doesn't warrant defending from the worst position at the table. I would be out of position post-flop with a hand that's often dominated or a coin flip against Minni's likely raising range. The pot odds (1.57:1) don't overcome the positional disadvantage and weak hand strength. Better to preserve my stack and wait for stronger opportunities where I have better position or hand values.

I hold 9d 8d in the big blind. Minni has raised to 400 from the button. 98s is a strong suited connector that plays well post-flop, especially in a heads-up situation. Although Minni has position on me, the pot odds (300 more to win a total pot that will be 850) and the playability of my hand make this a standard call to see a flop.

I have a gutshot straight draw (9-8 on a 10-6-4 board, needing a 7). I am out of position against the pre-flop aggressor, Minni. The standard play in this spot is to check to the raiser and see how they react. If Minni bets a reasonable amount, I may consider calling based on pot odds and implied odds, but for now, checking is the most sound tactical move.

I have premium pocket Aces (A♣ A♥) on a relatively dry flop 4♥ T♣ 6♥ and it’s heads-up after Flash checked. This is a clear spot to bet for value and to charge/price out heart draws and speculative hands (connectors, lower pocket pairs, single-heart combos). I’ll size up to ~2/3 pot (600 into 850): big enough to get called by worse hands (top pairs, medium pairs, broadways) and to make two-heart draws pay to continue, but not so large that I fold out almost every calling range or overcommit unnecessarily. The A♥ in my hand blocks some of the nut-heart combos, which reduces the frequency of strong two-heart draws. Plan: if called, re-evaluate on the turn and continue extracting value on safe turns; if check-raised small/medium, call and re-evaluate; if shoved very large, most of the time AA is still ahead and I’ll call given pot odds and effective stacks.

On a flop of 4h-Tc-6h, I am holding 9d-8d for a gutshot straight draw (needing a 7). Minni has bet 600 into a pot of 850, which is a significant sizing (approximately 70% pot). Given that I am out of position and only have a 4-outer (approx. 8.5% to hit on the turn), the pot odds of 3.4 to 1 are not sufficient to justify a call. Furthermore, I lack a backdoor flush draw or strong overcards. It is best to fold and preserve my stack for a more advantageous situation.

Raising to 250 from the button with 8sTs. This is 2.5x the BB and applies pressure to win the blinds. Given Minni's short stack at 7,295 chips, he's approaching push/fold territory. Flash may fold from the SB, giving me the blinds or a heads-up pot in position. This hand plays well in position post-flop if called. Standard aggression from the button with decent equity.

Holding K4o in the small blind against a button raise is a clear fold. While a King has some high card value, the hand is very weak, plays poorly out of position, and I would be forced to play the rest of the hand against Claude with a positional disadvantage. There is no need to defend such a wide range here.

K♠3♥ is a very weak, offsuit hand and is often dominated by a standard button opening range. Claude opened to 250 (2.5bb); the pot is 400 and it costs me 150 to call, so I’d need roughly 27% equity to break even. Versus a BTN raise (pairs, broadways, suited A/K/Q, some connectors) K3o usually has significantly less usable equity and terrible postflop playability (poor kicker, few straight/flush prospects). I could defend wider with suited kings or broadways or 3-bet with hands that have blocker value, but K3o is not one of them. With ~73BB behind I’d rather preserve my stack for better spots — fold here.

I am on the button with Ace-Four of clubs, which is a strong starting hand in a three-handed game. I have a positional advantage and a healthy stack. Standard play here is to open with a raise to put pressure on the blinds and build the pot. A 3x big blind raise is appropriate.

I have A♦4♥ in the small blind facing a standard button open to 300. A4o is a marginal hand with a weak kicker and poor post‑flop playability out of position, so 3‑betting here is too ambitious and folding surrenders too much equity for the small extra cost. Calling 250 more (300 total committed) is cheap to see a flop, keeps the pot controlled and preserves stack depth for situations where I actually connect. Plan: call now. If the big blind 3‑bets large (puts me in for a big portion of my 72BB stack, e.g. 3‑bet to ~900+), fold. If we go multi‑way, proceed very cautiously on flops without an Ace — continue only with top pair, strong draws or clear fold equity.

K♣T♠ is a solid hand from the big blind. I'm facing a 200-chip call to win a 700-chip pot, giving me 3.5:1 odds. This is a mathematically sound spot to see the flop in a multi-way pot. King-Ten offsuit has good playability post-flop—I can flop top pair, gutshots, or overcards. Given my position disadvantage (BB, worst post-flop), I'll keep the action simple with a call rather than escalating the pot with a raise. Flash's button raise is standard, and Minni's call suggests medium strength. I'll evaluate the flop texture before committing more chips.

This is a 3-way pot and I'm out of position with A♦4♥ on Qc 8h Th — I have only Ace-high and a very weak backdoor heart draw (need two hearts). The flop heavily favors the opener/button and BB's calling ranges (lots of Q/T/top pairs, broadways and heart draws). Betting here has little value and poor fold equity multiway; I'd mostly get called by better hands or face a raise putting me in a tough spot. I should control the pot and see a turn card. Plan: check. If I face a small continuation bet (roughly ≤1/3 pot ≈ ≤300) I'll consider calling to realize equity and try to hit an Ace/4 or pick up more equity on turn; I will fold to larger C-bets or any raise out of position. If it checks through I'll reassess on the turn and only continue with a pair, a strong draw, or clear improvement.

Middle pair (Tens) with K kicker and a 4-out gutshot straight draw on a moderately coordinated Q♣-8♥-T♥ flop. I'm out of position in a 3-way pot after Minni checked. Checking is the optimal play to control the pot, gather information about Flash's hand strength, and preserve flexibility. My current holding has reasonable equity, and checking allows me to respond strategically to Flash's action on this street while maintaining options for the turn.

I am on the button with Ace-high and a backdoor nut flush draw. The flop of Qc-8h-Th is quite coordinated and likely connects well with the ranges of both the Small Blind and Big Blind, who both called my pre-flop raise. Since both opponents have checked, I have the option to continuation bet or check back. In a three-way pot on such a wet board, a c-bet is less likely to succeed as a pure bluff. By checking back, I control the size of the pot, realize my equity for free, and can potentially improve on the turn with an Ace or a club. If I face a bet on the turn, I can re-evaluate based on the card and the size of the bet.

I have A♦4♥ in SB in a 3‑way pot (Flash opened, Claude cold‑called). On the Qc‑8h‑Th‑7c turn I still have no pair and no realistic draw — the backdoor heart flush is dead after the club on the turn and I only have 6 clean outs to pair on the river (3 Aces + 3 fours), which in a multiway pot is often second‑best. Betting OOP into two opponents who both checked is a low‑EV bluff: many hands that beat me (Qx, Tx, J9 straights, solid broadways) will call a probe bet cheaply, and I don’t want to inflate the pot out of position with a stone‑air hand. I planned to check the flop and only continue with a pair or a strong draw; that plan still holds. I’ll check the turn and see the river — fold to a sizable bet now, and only force action on the river with a clear improvement (paired Ace/4) or a very favorable board runout.

I have middle pair (Tens with King kicker) on a relatively dry Q-8-10-7 board. After both opponents checked on the flop and Minni checked the turn, this is a strong spot to take the initiative. My pair of Tens is likely the best hand, and betting puts pressure on any draws that missed or weaker holdings. A 600-chip bet (roughly 2/3 of the 900-chip pot) is appropriately sized to extract value while respecting the stacks. Flash's initial raise followed by a flop check, combined with Minni's passive checks (short-stack preservation mode), suggests both are weak here. Taking down this pot with a firm but not overcommittal bet is the right move.

On the turn, the 7 of clubs gives me the nut flush draw. Claude led out with a 600 bet into a 900 pot after everyone checked the flop. While the board is coordinated and straights like J9 are possible, I have reasonable pot odds and very strong implied odds to call. Since I am in position, I can see how the river develops; if I hit my flush, I can potentially stack an opponent holding a lesser hand like two-pair or a straight. Ace-high also retains some showdown value if the betting doesn't escalate further. I will call to realize my equity.

I have A♦4♥ in the small blind and currently have only Ace‑high on Qc‑8h‑Th‑7c. The turn (7♣) killed my backdoor heart possibility, so I have just 6 clean outs (three Aces, three 4s) ≈13% to improve on the river — and in a 3‑way pot a lot of those hits will still be second‑best. Claude’s turn raise (and Flash’s call) represents enough value/strength that continuing out of position is a low‑EV play. Calling 600 here gives poor effective odds and raising is nonsensical with no fold equity or real equity. I preserve stack depth for better spots and fold.

River value bet with pair of Tens and King kicker. Flash's betting pattern (flop check, turn call) suggests either a draw that missed the blank river card or a marginal pair. My hand is likely the strongest. A 700-chip bet extracts value from Flash's weaker holdings while remaining reasonable in size. Flash either calls with a worse pair or folds a missed draw, both profitable outcomes for my hand strength in this spot.

On the river, the 5 of spades is a complete blank that doesn't improve my hand. I have Ace-high with a missed nut flush draw. The pot is 2800 and Claude has bet 700, offering me 4-to-1 odds on a call. However, Claude led the turn into two players and followed through on the river, suggesting he has at least a pair or better. In a three-way pot that has seen significant action on the turn, Ace-high is rarely the best hand. While the price is tempting, my range has plenty of better hands to call with, and I have no real showdown value here. I will fold.

Button with K♣Q♦ (~70BB) — a top-tier opening hand in position. With only the two blinds left and the pot only 150, I want to seize initiative, isolate the blinds, and build a pot with positional advantage. A standard 3xBB open to 300 is appropriate: large enough to fold out weaker holdings and charge speculative calls but not so large to overcommit. Postflop plan: if called, play aggressively on K/Q/A-high and dry boards (c-bet/fold or turn‑barrel selectively); vs a small 3-bet I can call to use position, fold to a polarizing large 3-bet or 4‑bet shove depending on sizing and opponent tendencies.

A4 offsuit is a marginal hand from the small blind, and I'm out of position against an aggressive raiser (Minni). While Minni has demonstrated a loose, aggressive style throughout these 50 hands, A4o doesn't have sufficient equity to warrant a call or raise. The pot odds aren't compelling enough, and the risk of facing a 3-bet from Flash or getting into a difficult post-flop spot with marginal hand strength outweighs the potential reward. With a deep stack and 100 hands remaining, I should preserve chips for premium opportunities. This is a disciplined fold.

I am in the Big Blind with King-Ten offsuit (Ks Td). Minni has raised from the Button to 300. Given the pot odds (calling 200 to win 450) and the fact that KTo is a strong enough hand to defend against a standard Button opening range, I will call to see a flop. Raising would likely only result in being called or raised by better hands, and KTo plays reasonably well in a heads-up pot from the blinds.

The flop is Ac Jc Jh, giving me a gutshot straight draw (needing a Queen). This board is heavily flavored toward the pre-flop aggressor's (Minni) range, which includes many Ax, Jx, and broadway hands. Since I am out of position and have no made hand, checking is the standard play. I will evaluate based on the size of any potential bet Minni makes.

Flop: Jc Ac Jh. I hold Kc Qd — no pair, only backdoor club outs and very little equity against a BB caller. Flash’s calling range to my button open is heavy in Ax, Jx and pairs that this board hits hard, so a continuation bet here would be a thin bluff with poor fold equity and a high chance of being raised or called by better. I have position, so pot-control is optimal: check to see a turn, plan to fold to significant pressure unless a club/K/Q or another improving card appears, or to float a small probe bet and bluff later. Checking preserves chips and keeps the decision-making flexible.

The board is Ac Jc Jh 2h. I have Ks Td for a gutshot straight draw (Queen). Minni checked back the flop, which often indicates she doesn't have a strong hand (like an Ace or a Jack), though she could be trapping. By checking the turn, I can see a river card for free if she checks behind, or I can evaluate a bet based on the price. Since I only have King high and a gutshot, checking is the most prudent play to keep the pot small and realize my equity.

Pot 650, I’m in position with K♣Q♦ on J♣ A♣ J♥ 2♥. I have no pair and after the 2♥ the backdoor club possibility is dead (I would have needed both turn+river clubs). Flash’s flat-call range to my button open contains a lot of A‑x, J‑x and pocket pairs that crush KQ on this board, so my equity and fold equity are both poor. Flop and turn checks from Villain make a bluff less likely to succeed and a c‑bet here would commit chips with a marginal hand that’s easy to raise or call. Optimal play is pot control: check and see the river, folding to meaningful pressure. If Villain checks through I can re-evaluate on river for a cheap bluff or showdown if I pair.

On a board of Ac Jc Jh 2h 3s, the action has gone check-check on both the flop and the turn. This suggests that neither myself nor Minni has a particularly strong hand. I am holding King-high (Ks Td). While King-high is weak, it does have some showdown value against missed draws or lower non-paired hands. Betting here is unlikely to fold out better hands like small pocket pairs (44-99) which might still call a small bet, and I'm unlikely to get called by worse. Therefore, checking to reach a showdown for free is the most logical play in this small pot.

Board: Jc Ac Jh 2h 3s. I hold Kc Qd — no pair, my one-card club draw missed. Both players checked flop and turn and Flash checked the river, so his line is consistent with A‑x, J‑x, or medium pairs that beat me; he may also have missed holdings but checked back. My hand has almost no showdown value and very little fold equity against a checked line, so a river bluff is thin and likely to be called by the hands that beat me. Pot control is the right play: don’t invest more chips with a marginal hand. I will check and take the showdown (or fold to a large river bet if faced with pressure).

With 9d-3d on the button against two active opponents, this is an easy fold. 9-3 offsuit is one of the worst possible hands - it has no connectivity, no high card value, and will struggle post-flop. While button position is strong, it cannot overcome the fundamental weakness of this holding. Even against defender ranges from the blinds, this hand is likely in the bottom 5% of all possible holdings. I should conserve my chips for better opportunities.

I am in the Small Blind with 8d 6c, which is a weak semi-connected hand. Given that the Button has folded, it's just me and Minni in the Big Blind. However, calling the extra 50 to see a flop in a small pot is acceptable for speculative value, but usually, a fold is safer to maintain stack integrity against an opponent who often calls or raises pre-flop.

I am on the button with Ace-Seven offsuit (A7o) in a three-handed game. This is a standard opening hand. I will raise to 3x the big blind to take control of the pot and pressure the blinds. My stack size is healthy, and A7o is strong enough to play for a raise against the remaining two players.

Facing a standard 3x button open to 300 from the button. I’m in the SB with Qh Tc (Q‑10 offsuit). Pot is 450 and I must put in +250 to call, so my break-even equity is ~35.7% (250 / (450+250)). Folding is too tight; a min 3‑bet to 500 is thin — it buys little fold equity, commits chips out of position, and will be easily called. I’ll call 300 total to see a flop and keep the pot controllable. Postflop plan: proceed with top pair or good draws, use pot control/OOP caution if I miss, and fold to large 3‑bets or heavy action without strong equity.

I'm in the big blind with Qd4c - a weak, unconnected hand. Flash raised to 300 from the button and Minni called from the small blind. I'm facing two opponents out of position. 

Queen-Four offsuit has poor playability: it doesn't make premium hands, plays poorly in multiway pots, and is easily dominated. With two opponents already invested, my hand has minimal equity. The pot is laying me 3.5:1 odds (700 to call 200), but my hand is too weak to justify entering a three-way pot out of position.

I have a solid chip stack (17,880) and should be selective about which marginal hands I play. This is a clear fold - I'll preserve my stack and wait for stronger opportunities.

I have top pair (T) with a Queen kicker on a very dry 2c-5h-Th flop. I’m out of position vs a BTN open that’s likely fairly wide, so this is a spot to both get value from worse tens/one-pair hands and deny equity to backdoor draws. I’ll make a half-pot protection/value bet (~350 into 700) — big enough to charge/price out many floats and worse pairs but small enough to keep the pot manageable OOP. If I face a large raise I’ll seriously consider folding; if he calls I’ll re-evaluate on the turn and proceed cautiously.

I raised pre-flop from the button with Ace-high and was called by Minni in the small blind. The flop comes Ten-high, and Minni leads out with a 'donk bet' of 350 into a 650 pot. This lead often represents a marginal Ten, a medium pocket pair, or a draw. With Ace-high and position, I have enough equity and potential to call. Folding would be too tight given the pot odds and my position, while raising might overplay my hand against a range that could include a Ten. I will call to see how the turn develops.

I bet the flop for value and protection and got called. Turn brings an A — a very relevant overcard that turns many of Flash’s reasonable continuing hands (Ax, A5/A2, sometimes A‑T, even some two‑pair/sets) into clear winners. I’m OOP with only T‑pair, Q kicker; I don’t want to build a big pot with a marginal made hand against a deeper stack or get check‑raised off. Checking controls the pot size, avoids difficult decisions vs a turn raise, and lets me put the decision back on him. Plan: check and fold to a large or polarizing bet (raise), call small/medium bluffs or thin value bets, and if he checks back I’ll re‑evaluate the river for a small value bet or showdown depending on the card.

I have turned top pair with Ace-Seven. The Ace is a very good card for my range, and Minni's check after donking the flop suggests she might be wary of the Ace or was semi-bluffing. I will bet for value and protection against heart draws or straight draws. A bet of 700 (half pot) is appropriate to extract value from a Ten or a smaller pocket pair while not overcommitting if she is trapping.

I have top pair (T) with a Queen kicker, out of position vs the BTN. I bet the flop for value/protection and got called; the turn Ace is a very relevant overcard that improves many of Flash’s continuing hands (Ax, A5/A2, A10, some two‑pair). Flash’s line — open BTN, call flop bet, smallish turn bet (700 into 2100) — is consistent with a mixed range: some Ax/value hands, some worse tens, and some thinner bluffs. Calling 700 into a pot that will be 2800 gives me ~25% break‑even equity (700/2800). QTo still has >25% equity vs a realistic continuing range (it beats worse tens and many non‑Ace hands and can get to showdown), so folding is too tight. Raising here OOP into an Ace on the turn would bloat the pot with a marginal hand and buy little fold equity — it’s too risky. Plan: call the 700 to control the pot and see the river. On the river I’ll re‑evaluate: call small/medium bets for showdown, and fold to large polarizing overbets that represent two‑pair/sets/Ax combos that dominate me.

Board: 2c 5h Th As Jc. I hold Tc Qh — top pair with a vulnerable Q kicker. Flash’s line (BTN open, call flop, bet 700 on the turn) is weighted to Ax, A5/A2, A10, and some two‑pair/sets; it can also include some thin value or bluffs, but the turn bet strongly polarizes toward hands that beat me. He checked the river, which reads as pot‑control with showdown value (most likely Ax or a weaker two‑pair/paired jack) rather than a fold‑inducing spot. Betting the river for thin value risks getting raised or called by better hands (Ax, Jx) and only extracts a small amount from worse tens — while folding out some worse hands that would pay me off. Given the pot (2800), my stack (6045) and the marginal nature of my kicker, the lowest‑variance, highest‑EV line is to check and go to showdown rather than bloat the pot or face a river raise. I’ll check and see the showdown.

On the river, the Jack of Clubs completes some potential gutshot draws like Queen-Ten, and could have improved a hand like Jack-Ten to two pair. I have top pair with a weak kicker (Seven). Checking back allows me to realize my showdown value and win the pot against her missed draws and weaker pairs without risking a check-raise or value-cutting myself.

I’m on the button with 9♠8♠ — a suited connector with strong playability and position against only the blinds. With a ~60bb stack this is a good hand to open and apply pressure. AJ-offsuit is a premium hand. Minni is short-stacked at 5,745 and raised from the button to 300. I am in the Big Blind with Kd 6d. Minni opened from the Button to 300, and Claude 3-bet from the Small Blind to 750. I hold AJ on a QQ7 flop—a very strong hand (top kicker with the board pair). I'm first to act on the flop with Minni severely short-stacked at 4545 chips and Flash folded from the hand.

Strategic reasoning:
1. **Hand strength**: My AJ gives me the best kicker with the Queens on board. Against Minni's pre-flop raising range, I'm likely ahead of most hands (small pairs, missed broadway cards).

2. **Positional advantage**: I'm first to act, allowing me to define the action and apply pressure.

3. **Opponent short stack**: Minni's stack-to-pot ratio is tight (about 2.8x pot remaining). A solid bet puts significant pressure on her decision-making without overcommitting myself if she shoves.

4. **Continuation bet sizing**: Betting 1000 (roughly 62% of the 1600 pot) is a standard, well-balanced c-bet that:
   - Builds value with my strong hand
   - Charges any draws (unlikely to exist on this dry board)
   - Creates a manageable scenario if called
   - Avoids over-betting and giving up value

5. **Minni's likely holdings**: She raised on the button and called my 3-bet, suggesting decent holdings, but the QQ7 board has likely whiffed many hands in her range.

This aggressive but measured approach maximizes value while maintaining good pot control.

Flop (Qc Qh 7d) I missed completely with 9s8s — no pair, no immediate flush/straight draw (only backdoor outs). In position with a speculative suited connector, calling is the lowest-variance, highest-optional play: I can see a turn, use my position to fold to heavy aggression, or barrel if I pick up real equity. A raise would be a pure bluff with poor blockers and risks a shove or a call by many Qx/overpairs; folding is too tight given the price and playability. Plan: call to realize equity and control the pot on future streets; fold to a large turn bet if I don’t improve or re-evaluate if I pick up a pair/draw.

AJ offsuit with top kicker on a dry paired board (QQ75) is a premium holding. Minni is severely short-stacked with only 3,295 chips remaining after calling my flop bet. Raising with AcKd on the button in 3-handed play. This is a premium hand that should be played aggressively for value. Call to 350 (put in 250 more). J♠6♥ is marginal but defendable from the big blind vs a standard Button open to 3.5bb. Continuation bet on a dry flop after pre-flop raise. Minni's check shows weakness, and I have positional advantage. Standard C-bet sizing of 60-65% pot applies pressure with good fold equity while maintaining pot control with premium hand (AK). This is standard aggressive poker in position.

Fold. Claude's flop bet is 480 into a 1230 pot (bet ≈0.6 pot). Queen-Five offsuit is a marginal hand that typically falls outside of a standard opening range from the Button, especially when opponents in the blinds have shown willingness to defend or 3-bet. I'm in the small blind with Q♠4♣. Pot is 150 and it costs me 50 to complete (total commit 100) — good pot odds (need ~25% equity) to see a flop. Check. I’ve flopped a weak pair (4s) with Q♠4♣ on K♠6♠4♦ — second pair with a poor kicker and I’m out of position to Claude (deep and aggressive). With 2-10 offsuit on a Ks 6s 4d board, I have essentially no hand strength—no pair, no draw, no equity. Minni checked from the small blind, indicating weakness. I check. I have Q♠4♣ — only second pair (board pair of 4s) with a very poor kicker and I'm out of position to a deep, aggressive Claude. With 2s Td on a Ks 6s 4d 3s board, I have no equity whatsoever—no pair, no draw, no possibility of improvement. I’ll check to showdown. I have Q♠4♣ — just a single pair of 4s with a weak kicker. This is a straightforward check on the river. I'm holding 2s-Td, which has zero equity against any reasonable hand. The board (Ks 6s 4d 3s 9c) contains no pair for me and no draws have connected. 

Minni has checked the flop, turn, and now is checking to me on the river again. This pattern indicates weakness, but my hand is too weak to bluff profitably. With a stack of 20,030 and Minni short at 3,845, pot control is important - betting here would be burning chips with a worthless hand. 

Checking allows me to see showdown cheaply and conserve chips. My hand cannot win through betting, so the only rational play is to check and minimize losses by seeing the showdown.

I’m on the button with J♦9♦ — a suited, connected hand that plays very well in position. 8-5 offsuit is a weak hand with poor playability, especially out of position in the small blind. I'm on the button with 4s-Tc against two blinds. While this is a weak hand, I'm getting 3:1 pot odds to call (150/100). Check. I have 6♠5♠ — no pair on K♠4♥2♥ and only backdoor draws (need runner-runner spades for a flush) plus a single-card straight (any 3 completes 2-3-4-5-6). Fold. I’m out of position with 6♠5♠ on K♠-4♥-2♥ — I have no pair and only very weak runner-runner draws. I am on the button with QJ offsuit, which is a strong opening hand in three-handed play. My goal is to raise to take control of the pot and potentially win the blinds. I will open for a standard 3x raise.

I'm in the big blind with K4o against Flash's button raise. After Minni folded, it's heads-up. I need to call 200 to win 450 (2.25:1 pot odds). Checking with K4o on 3h Kd 3s against an aggressive button raiser. Top pair is playable but the weak kicker and paired board make this a cautious spot. This builds the pot while protecting my hand and controlling the narrative against Flash's aggression.

Claude has led into me on the turn after calling a continuation bet on a very dry board (K-3-3). My hand, Queen-Jack high, has no showdown value and very little equity against his range, which likely includes a King or a pocket pair. Given that I have no draw and the pot odds do not justify chasing a marginal pair that might not even be best, the most disciplined play is to fold and preserve my stack.

Button with J-T offsuit (Th Jd) — a clear open-raise candidate in 3-handed play. I have ~40 BB, good position on both blinds, and decent post‑flop playability. Calling 100 is passive and surrenders initiative; raising wins the blinds often and lets me play the hand in position when called. I size to 300 (≈3x BB) to get respectable fold equity, avoid bloating pots unnecessarily, and keep flexibility post‑flop. Plan: if I get folds I collect the blinds; if I get one caller I’ll play post‑flop aggressively in position (c‑bet favorable textures, pot‑control on dry boards, probe for info). If Claude (SB) 3‑bets large, I’ll fold JTo; versus a small 3‑bet I may call and proceed with positional advantage. This sizing balances value and protection against isolation 3‑bets given stack depths and opponents’ tendencies.

2-10 offsuit from small blind is a weak hand that doesn't warrant a call against a button raise. While Minni is short-stacked (3745), his button raise to 300 is standard, and I need a much stronger range to defend from small blind. This hand has poor playability post-flop and limited win potential. With a strong stack (20730), I can afford to fold weak hands and wait for premium opportunities. Folding weak hands from out of position is fundamental poker strategy.

I am in the big blind with Qh 5d. Minni, on the button, has raised to 300. With a stack of only 3,745, Minni is relatively short compared to the other stacks. Qh 5d is a weak hand to defend with, especially against a button raise which can be wide but still typically stronger than this hand. Folding is the most prudent play to conserve chips as I'm out of position and have a hand that doesn't play well post-flop.

J3 offsuit is a weak hand with poor equity against the blinds' likely calling ranges. With limited hand strength, a poor kicker, and insufficient pot odds, folding is the standard play. I'll preserve my strong stack position and wait for better opportunities.

I am in the Small Blind with K-10 of clubs, a strong suited king that plays well multi-way and heads-up. With only 3 players at the table and the button having folded, it's a battle between the blinds. Minni (Big Blind) has a relatively short stack (about 41 BBs). I will raise to 300 to put pressure on Minni and potentially win the pot pre-flop or see a flop with the initiative and a strong hand.

Facing a 3x open to 300 from the small blind while I'm in the big blind with 8♠4♥. This is a very weak, unsuited, poorly-connected hand that will be dominated a lot and plays terribly postflop. Calling 200 (300 total) only gets ~33% pot odds, but my realized equity versus a typical SB open is well below that and I’ll be in a tough out-of-position spot multi-street. Raising would be a fancy bluff with little fold equity against this deeper-stacked opponent. With ~40bb effective I’ll preserve chips for better spots and fold.

I am on the button with 5-4 offsuit. While it is a potential hand to open-raise as a steal in some configurations, 54o is generally too weak to play profitably against active opponents like Claude and a relatively short-stacked Minni who may be looking to shove or defend aggressively. Folding is the most disciplined and standard play here.

5♠8♦ (5h8d) is a weak offsuit hand and I’ll be out of position vs Claude. However the price to see a flop is tiny — 50 more chips into a 150 pot (I’d be 100 total committed). Raising to 200 as a bluff is exploitative but unnecessary and likely to be called by a deep BB, while folding surrenders the blind for free. I’ll take the cheap flop and see if I can hit or pick up fold equity on later streets; plan to fold to heavy aggression unless I hit meaningful equity (pair, two pair, strong draw).

Holding Js Td in the big blind against only a small blind call from a short-stacked opponent. With two opponents and weak action, checking to see the flop in position is optimal. This is a solid hand that doesn't require immediate aggression, and I can play more profitably with positional advantage on subsequent streets.

I have 5♥8♦ on 6♣9♣5♠ — bottom pair (pair of 5s with an 8 kicker). This is a vulnerable but real piece of the board: many hands (9x, 6x, overcards that pair on turn, and club draws) beat me and there are backdoor/flush possibilities. I planned pre-flop to see a cheap flop and control the pot out of position. Betting here would bloated the pot and commit me against range advantage/club draws; checking keeps the pot small and lets me call a reasonable bet or fold to big aggression. If I improve (7 for a straight or trips) I can get aggressive; otherwise I’ll proceed cautiously. Check to pot-control and evaluate the turn.

I'm in position against a short-stacked opponent who checked the flop. With J-T on a 6-9-5 board, I have two overcards giving me reasonable equity and showdown value. Betting 100 into the 200 pot applies pressure on Minni's short stack while remaining reasonable. This is a profitable spot to take down the pot or build it with a hand that has decent equity if called.

Flop: 6♣ 9♣ 5♠ — I hold 5♥8♦: a vulnerable pair of fives with a weak kicker, out of position. Claude’s raise to 100 is small (~1/3 pot) and looks like a standard c-bet or probe with a wide range (club draws, 6x/9x, some overcards or small pairs). My preflop plan was pot control and to fold to heavy aggression without improvement. Raising here would bloat the pot out of position with a marginal hand and is unnecessarily combative; folding surrenders a made hand for free. I’ll call the 100 to keep the pot manageable, see the turn, and re-evaluate — folding to large turn/river aggression unless I improve to two pair/trips or the turn changes the draw dynamics (e.g., a safe card that blanks club draws).

Board is 6♣ 9♣ 5♠ 6♠ and I hold 5♥8♦ — a vulnerable bottom pair with a weak kicker. Claude’s small flop raise was likely a wide probe (club draws, 6x/9x, small pairs), and the turn pairing the board makes trips (6x) a real possibility. Betting here gains little value (few worse hands call) and a small ‘block’ bet simply opens the door to a raise that I don’t want to face out of position. I’ll control the pot and keep my options: check and evaluate Claude’s action. I’ll fold to sizable aggression on the turn, only call tiny probes if they look like bluffs, and re-evaluate on the river — only get aggressive if I improve to two pair/trips or get a clear read.

Betting 175 into the 400 pot on the turn with J-T after Minni's two consecutive checks. Minni's weak action (two checks with a short stack) indicates vulnerability. My hand has ~15 outs for river improvement and reasonable equity if called. This bet applies pressure on the short stack while maintaining profitable value. If Minni folds, I take down the pot; if called, I have decent winning chances on the river.

I have 5♥8♦ — a vulnerable bottom pair on 6♣ 9♣ 5♠ 6♠, out of position against Claude. He’s shown a small flop probe (100) and a small turn bet (175 into a 575 pot) — sizes that indicate a wide, potentially blocking/relatively weak continuing range (club draws, floats, some 6x/9x, small pairs). Raising OOP with this marginal hand is unnecessary and would bloat the pot when I’m likely behind to any 6 or 9 and crushed by sets/trips. Folding now is too passive given the cheap price to see the river. I have 5 clean outs (3 eights + 2 fives) to make two pair or trips (~11% to improve), and the bet gives me reasonable fold options on the river if he shows strength.

Call 175 to keep the pot controlled and realize equity. Plan: if I improve to two pair/trips I’ll extract value; if the river doesn’t help and Claude fires a sizable bet, I’ll fold; small river bet/check showdown I can evaluate then.

River snapshot: I hold 5♥8♦ and the board is 6♣ 9♣ 5♠ 6♠ 5♦ — I’ve made a full house (fives full of sixes). Only very specific holdings beat me (any 6 in Claude’s hand or the lone remaining 5 for quads). Claude’s line — checking pre, a small flop c-bet and a small turn bet — looks like a wide continuing range (floats, single pair like 9x, some 6x, maybe club floats). Given the limited number of combos that beat me (~10–15% of a natural continuing range) and the likelihood he’s got many hands I dominate, a value bet is the correct play. I want a size that gets called by worse two-pair/one-pair hands and bluffs but doesn’t overcommit me if Claude unexpectedly towers with the few hands that beat me. Betting ~300 into a 750 pot (~0.4 pot) achieves that balance — it extracts value from 9x, small pocket pairs and weaker made hands while keeping the pot controllable. If he calls I win good value; if he raises big I’ll re-evaluate (but given how few combos beat me I’m willing to call reasonable raises).

Analyzing the river situation: I hold J-T high on a board of 6c 9c 5s 6s 5d (paired board with 6-5). Minni's action pattern is significant - checking both the flop and turn, then suddenly raising 300 on the river after calling my bets. This is a strong indication of a made hand rather than a bluff, especially given that the river card (5d) completed the paired board with dangerous holdings available (pairs of 6s, 5s, 9s, or any ace/king).

Pot odds analysis: I'm asked to call 300 into a pot of 1050, giving me approximately 4.5:1 odds. This requires approximately 18% equity to break even. However, my J-T high has very limited value on this particular runout. Against Minni's likely value-heavy range (pairs, high cards), I'm estimated to have closer to 10-15% equity, which falls short of breakeven.

Minni is short-stacked (3420), and while short stacks can be aggressive, the specific action pattern here (check-call, check-call, then river raise) is classic for a hand that either made a pair or had limited value on previous streets but found a reason to bet the river. My unpaired hand doesn't connect with the board and I'm very unlikely to be ahead. Folding preserves my chip lead and avoids a marginal spot where I'm likely behind.

I’m on the button with K♥3♠ and 44.7bb effective. This is a steal spot in a 3-handed game — K3o is marginal but has fold equity and I act last postflop. Limping (calling 100) is too passive and invites cheap multiway pots; a tiny min-raise (200) will invite too much defense. I’ll raise to a standard open (300) to apply pressure and play in position. Plan: if called I’ll c-bet on dry, favorable flops and fold to strong resistance unless I improve; fold to a large 3-bet (respect the stacks and avoid flipping with a weak hand).

Q-6 offsuit is a weak hand that doesn't warrant involvement, especially facing a likely 3-way pot. Minni's button raise shows a wide range, and my hand is below average. With a healthy 20K stack, folding to preserve chips for premium situations is optimal. Better spots will come.

I am holding AQo in the Big Blind, which is a very strong hand in a three-handed game. Minni opened from the Button, and since she has a relatively short stack (around 42 BBs), I want to put pressure on her and define the hand. A 3-bet is standard here to take the initiative and potentially win the pot pre-flop or isolate her range.

I opened to 300 on the button with K♥3♠ aiming to steal. Flash in the BB 3-bets to 1000, leaving the pot at 1350 and me facing a 700 call. K3o is poorly positioned against a BB 3‑bet range (AK/AQ/Kx and pairs) and is frequently dominated; calling 700 into 1350 gives only ~34% pot odds but my hand has much less equity vs that range. 4-betting/isolation with K3 is a marginal, high-variance play I don’t want to make with ~41.7bb effective. I’ll preserve my stack and fold to the large 3‑bet.

Folding K3 from the button. While position is advantageous, this hand is too weak to justify building a pot. K3 plays poorly out of position if called, and with a healthy 20k stack, there's no need to force marginal situations. Better spots will come.

I am in the Small Blind with 9c 4d, a weak hand. Claude has already folded from the button. The pot is small (150) and it costs me only 50 to call, but playing 94o out of position against Minni is generally unprofitable. Given the high stack depth relative to Minni's remaining chips, I'll simply fold and wait for a better spot rather than trying to complete or raise with air.
