I am on the button with T3o. This is a very weak starting hand. Minni in the Small Blind and Claude in the Big Blind have been playing reasonably active. With a 1.5bb investment already for the blinds and a short-stacked player in Minni (approx 42bb), there is little incentive to play this hand profitably from the button. Folding is the standard and most disciplined play here.

Heads-up from the small blind with 7♥8♦ — a playable, connected hand in a two-player pot. Pot is 150, I need 50 to call. I’ll raise to 200 total (standard 2x BB open). Raising here takes the initiative, applies pressure to the BB and wins the pot outright a fair amount of the time. It’s a small, controlled portion of my 4,170 stack so I can play postflop without being overcommitted. Plan: if Claude folds I scoop the 150. If he calls I’ll c‑bet on dry/ace-high flops and proceed cautiously on coordinated/wet boards; fold to a large 3‑bet.

Calling 100 more with pocket fours in heads-up against short-stacked opponent. Good implied odds with strong equity in 2-player situation. Chip advantage makes this a good spot to see a flop and potentially extract value if I hit a set. Pot odds are favorable at 4:1.

I raised preflop with 7♥8♦ and flopped a pair of eights on Ac 6c 8s. I have initiative as the preflop aggressor but the board is Ace‑high and two clubs, which hits a lot of the big‑blind’s calling range (many A‑x and club draws). Standard play is a continuation bet to get value from worse pairs and charge club draws while keeping the pot manageable. I’ll bet ~50% pot (200). Size is big enough to deny equity to backdoor clubs and get value from hands like 66/55/44 and some Kx, but small enough that I can fold to a large check‑raise (indicating an Ace or a set). If called I’ll proceed cautiously on the turn and re‑evaluate; if Claude raises big I’ll fold.

Calling with pocket fours on Ac 6c 8s. I have strong implied odds with a set and significant chip advantage over the short-stacked Minni. Getting 4:1 on the call makes this a mathematically sound play. If I hit my set on the turn or river, I can potentially stack Minni completely. Minni's aggressive pre-flop and flop action could represent a wide range, so my pair likely has decent equity. The favorable pot odds and set-mining potential make calling the best play here.

I hold 7♥8♦ and the turn paired the 8 (board Ac 6c 8s 8h) giving me trips. Claude called my flop c‑bet, so his range is weighted toward A‑x, some medium pairs (66, 55, 44), and missed backdoor club combos — most of which are behind my trips. The only hands that beat me on the turn are very specific (A8 with the remaining 8c giving a full house, or the unlikely quads), so this is a clear value‑bet spot. I’ll lead for value and protection OOP and keep the size moderate so Ax and pocket pairs will call but a check‑raise would credibly represent a full house/quads and allow me to fold. Betting ~50% pot (400) extracts value now while managing risk; if called I’ll re‑evaluate the river, if I face a large check‑raise I’ll fold to avoid committing with the small frequency of full houses/quads.

Folding pocket fours on this turn. The board has now paired up twice (8s 8h), and Minni's aggressive turn raise to 400 after the second eight hits is a strong signal of hand strength. Minni likely has an eight (making two pair or better), an ace, or a strong hand. My pocket fours have only 4 outs to the remaining 4s for a full house, giving me approximately 8% equity - far below the 25% needed to justify the call with these pot odds. While I have a chip advantage, calling here with such weak equity is not profitable in the long run. The 400 to call on a 1200 pot (3:1) doesn't compensate for my poor equity position. Folding minimizes losses and preserves chips for better spots.

Preflop, on the button with Qh8c in a three-handed pot this is a marginal but perfectly playable hand given position and stack depth (~46bb). Calling 100 gets decent pot odds (100 into a 150 pot ≈ 40% break-even equity) and preserves flexibility — I can see a flop cheaply and use position to realize equity or apply pressure. A min-raise to 200 is an option, but a tiny raise here often just induces 3-bets from deep opponents or folds out hands I want to keep; I prefer to keep the pot controllable and play postflop. Plan: call, evaluate the flop — value bet or continue if I pair the Q/8 or pick up good draws; fold or fold to heavy aggression if I miss and the action becomes hostile.

KJ offsuit is a solid broadway hand worthy of play from the small blind. Minni called 100 from the button, which is consistent with a wide, positional range. The pot is 250 and I need to call just 50 more, giving me 5:1 odds. This is a straightforward spot - KJ plays well in position against Minni's likely range, and I can make decisions post-flop with good stack depth. A simple call allows me to see what the big blind does while maintaining flexibility going forward.

I have King-Nine of hearts in the big blind. Both the button (Minni) and the small blind (Claude) have limped into the pot. Limping usually suggests a capped or weaker range. Since I have a strong suited hand that likely leads their limping ranges, I am raising to 500 to take the initiative, build the pot, and potentially win the blinds and limps immediately.

Facing a 5x raise to 500 after I already called 100, I’m going to call to 500 (add 400). Pot is 700; calling 400 into a 1100 pot requires ~36% equity — reasonable given Q♣8♣’s playability from the button. I keep the pot controllable and retain positional advantage against the raiser and the small blind who already called. A 3-bet to 900 is too committal and unnecessary with a marginal hand; folding surrenders good pot odds. Postflop plan: use position to evaluate texture — continue aggressively when I hit top pair or strong draws, c-bet selectively on favorable flops, and fold to heavy resistance when I miss. Calling preserves flexibility and the ability to extract value or fold cheaply on unfavorable boards.

Calling 400 into a 1100 chip pot gives 2.75:1 odds for KJ offsuit, which is acceptable. KJ plays well post-flop in multiway situations, especially with Minni's short-stacked presence (4120). Good pot odds + solid hand strength + stack depth makes this a straightforward call. I can extract value post-flop when I catch pieces or find strong spots to exploit the action.

KdJs is a strong broadway hand but has no made hand value on this dry 2c-5d-3c flop. The board is uncoordinated with minimal draw possibilities. I'm first to act postflop in a three-way pot against Flash (aggressive, raised preflop from BB) and short-stacked Minni (4120). Checking is optimal here because: (1) KJ is too weak to lead - I'd essentially be bluffing with air when two opponents likely have stronger holdings; (2) checking allows me to see Flash's action and leverage his likely aggression; (3) I maintain a strong hand that can improve on turn/river; (4) I control the pot size and preserve flexibility. If Flash bets, I can call with position and decent equity while keeping stacks in mind. If Flash checks through, I can reassess the turn dynamics. This is proper pot control with a broadway hand that needs to catch or slowplay to extract value.

I was the pre-flop aggressor with a raise to 500. The flop comes 2c-5d-3c, which is a low and somewhat connected board. While this board can hit a limper's range, my King-high still has some showdown value against complete air, and a continuation bet (C-bet) is standard here to represent a strong overpair or a big Ace. I will bet 600 (40% of the pot) to try and take the pot down immediately. If I face significant resistance, I can easily fold as my hand has very little equity to improve if behind.

I have Q♥8♣ on 2♣-5♦-3♣ — two overcards and a backdoor club draw (3 clubs including my 8♣). Flash was the preflop aggressor and barreled the flop for 600; Claude checked, so there’s room to maneuver. Calling 600 into a 2,100 pot gives me ~22% break-even equity. My chance to pair either hole card by the river (~24%) plus the slim backdoor flush/runner potential (~5%) puts my raw improvement equity above the break-even threshold, and I have position to fold on later streets if I miss. I won’t raise — my hand lacks fold equity and I don’t want to bloat the pot facing two deep stacks with just overcards. Plan: call to see the turn; if I pair a Queen or pick up a meaningful club/other improvement I’ll proceed aggressively for value, but if I completely miss and face a sizable turn bet I’ll fold to preserve my stack.

KJ has reasonable equity on this dry 2-5-3 board against likely opponent ranges. Getting 5.5:1 odds with two overcards and broadway potential. The dry flop is actually favorable for my hand - reduces the likelihood opponents flopped strong holdings. Minni's short stack means action will likely resolve, and I can reassess turn dynamics. Deep stacks allow for postflop flexibility. This is a straightforward call with decent pot odds and equity.

I've hit top pair (Kings with Jack kicker) on this dry 2c-5d-3c-Kc board - a significant improvement from my flop holding of two unpaired overcards. This is a strong hand that should be bet for value. The uncoordinated board makes strong opponent holdings unlikely, and I should capitalize on this favorable situation. A 1000 chip bet builds the pot while keeping stack depth reasonable, gets value from weaker holdings, pressures the short-stacked Minni, and maintains my positional advantage. This is straightforward value betting with top pair on a dry board.

The King of clubs on the turn is a significant card that grants me top pair. Claude's decision to lead out for 1,000 (a donk bet) after calling on the flop is a bit unusual. This could represent a flush, a straight (A-4), or perhaps a King that he just hit. However, given that I am getting over 4-to-1 pot odds (calling 1,000 to win 4,300), I cannot fold top pair here. Raising is too risky as it would bloat the pot against hands that beat me (flushes/straights). Calling allows me to evaluate the river while keeping the pot at a reasonable size.

Call 1000. On the turn I have Q♥8♣ on 2♣-5♦-3♣-K♣: I picked up a four-card club flush draw (9 club outs) plus backdoor pair outs (Qs/Qd and the three remaining 8s) — unique raw outs are high (~14) so my chance to improve on the river comfortably exceeds the ~16% break-even offered by calling 1000 into a 5300 pot. I also have positional advantage (button) so calling keeps the pot controllable and lets me see the river last. Opponents’ actions (Claude betting 1000, Flash calling) are consistent with Kx, club draws, or marginal pairs; a raise here would commit too many chips with a drawing hand and would often be called by better hands or 3‑bet by the deep stacks. Folding would surrender excellent pot odds. Plan: call to see the river; if I make the flush or a clean pair I’ll extract value, but if I miss and face significant river aggression I’ll be prepared to fold.

River bet with trip Kings (Kd-Js on 2c-5d-3c-Kc-Ks board). This is an extremely strong hand and I'm first to act on a completed river. Flash's calling pattern through the hand suggests they have some holding - likely a pair or broadway cards. If Flash has a King, my Jack kicker wins. Betting 2000 into the 6300 pot (1/3 pot sizing) extracts value from their likely calling range while appearing natural. No reason to check and allow them to get away cheap - I have the nuts-adjacent hand and should maximize value on this river.

On the river, the board has paired the Kings, giving me triple Kings with a Nine kicker. Claude's lead on the turn (1,000) and the river (2,000) is a very strong line, often indicating a straight, a flush, or potentially a full house (like 55, 33, or 22). However, with the board pairing the King on the river, the likelihood of Claude holding the final King is reduced. While I am behind a flush or a straight, I am getting approximately 4-to-1 pot odds (calling 2,000 to win a total pot of 10,300). Folding triple Kings in this spot, especially when the top card pairs, would be too exploitable. I will call to see the showdown, as my hand beats all of Claude's potential bluffs and non-King pairs, though I must be wary of Minni behind me who is short-stacked.

I missed the river — Q♥8♣ on 2♣-5♦-3♣-K♣-K♠ is just queen-high. On the turn I had a four-card club draw, but the river blanked that. Claude has led the last two streets (turn bet then river raise) and Flash was the preflop raiser who’s been calling down; that line is far more consistent with Kx/trips or other made hands than with pure bluffs. The pot is 10,300 and it costs me 2,000 to call (I’d need ~16% equity), but calling would commit ~79% of my stack (2,000 of 2,520) with essentially zero showdown outs. An all‑in raise to 2,520 only risks an extra 520 with very little fold equity versus two deep players who already showed willingness to call. Folding preserves my stack for better spots — I’m beaten too often here to justify calling or jamming.

Seven-deuce is the worst possible hand in poker with no playability from any position. Despite being on the button with a healthy stack and short stacks opposite me, this hand has no equity development, no premium card combinations, and cannot win any close decisions. Folding is the only correct action here - preserving chips for better opportunities is the fundamental principle of poker. No positional advantage justifies playing this hand.

I am holding 7h 2s, which is the worst possible starting hand in Texas Hold'em. Although I am in the Small Blind and only need to call 50 more to see the flop, playing this hand out of position against the Big Blind is generally unprofitable, especially since Minni has shown aggression in previous hands. Folding is the most prudent play to conserve my stack.

I have 5c 2s on the button. This is a very weak hand. While I am in position, the hand has almost no value and Minni, the small blind, has a relatively short stack (2520), which makes stealing less effective if she decides to shove or play back at me. I'll fold this hand.

I’ll call to 100 total. 6♦7♠ is a marginal, unsuited connector and I’m out of position against a big-stacked, often-aggressive Claude. Calling costs only 50 more into a 150 pot (very cheap) and keeps the pot manageable. Raising to 200 risks bloating the pot and getting 3‑bet or called by better hands; with this holding I prefer to see a flop cheaply and evaluate. Plan: continue only with meaningful connection (pair, two pair, open-ender/strong combo draws); otherwise check/fold to substantial aggression.

Short-stacked opponent (2,470 chips) limped from small blind. With Kc2h in the big blind, I should raise to apply pressure and potentially win the pot preflop or take down a favorable heads-up situation. K2o has enough value against a limping range, especially with positional advantage post-flop if called.

I’m on the small blind with 6d7s facing Claude’s raise to 400 (pot 500, I must call 300). Calling requires ~37.5% equity — far too rich for an offsuit, weak connector OOP that will be dominated by a typical raiser’s range and struggles to realize equity multi‑street. Raising would be overly loose and needlessly bloats the pot against a big, aggressive stack. My stack is 2470 and I should avoid marginal, OOP spots; I’ll fold and wait for a better opportunity.

Button with 6♦6♥ — a playable medium pocket pair. With only the blinds to act and ~24.7BB effective for me, I want to take the initiative, thin the field, and steal the blinds or play heads‑up where pocket pairs perform well. A standard 3xBB open (300 total) gives good fold equity and keeps my post‑flop decisions manageable. Plan: if I get one caller I’ll proceed for pot control and look to seize the initiative on favorable flops; if I face a large 3‑bet that commits a big chunk of my stack I’ll fold (66 is a poor dog vs a polarized 3‑bet), but I’ll call smallish 3‑bets and proceed cautiously.

I'm holding Th-3c in the Small Blind, which is a weak hand. While Minni is short-stacked with only 2,170 chips (7 BB), his raise to 300 from the Button still represents a reasonable raise sizing. Th-3c is well below the threshold for entering the pot, especially when facing a raise. Even considering Minni's short-stack status, my hand has minimal showdown value and poor playability post-flop. With a large chip stack (26,155), I can afford to wait for better opportunities. The correct play is to fold and preserve my strong position.

I am holding a strong pocket pair (99) in the Big Blind. The Button (Minni) has raised to 300 and is playing on a short stack of about 21.7 BBs. Given the position and the stack sizes, my pocket pair is well ahead of a typical button opening range. I will 3-bet to 900 to put pressure on Minni, potentially forcing a fold or setting up a situation where we can play for the rest of their chips on most flops.

I opened to 300 on the button with 6♦6♥ looking to steal/blind-play or set‑mine heads‑up. Flash (BB) 3‑bet to 900, which commits a large portion of my 2,170 stack (~40%). Calling another 600 requires ~32% equity to justify set‑mining, and with this stack depth implied odds are limited. Flash has shown aggressive 3‑betting and large postflop lines in previous hands, so his range is likely polarized/strong enough that 66 is a poor dog. My preflop plan was to fold to a large, stack‑committing 3‑bet. I preserve my stack for better spots and fold here.

Folding 4s9h on the button. While position is beneficial, this hand is too weak to profitably play against two opponents. The pot odds of 1.5:1 don't compensate for the marginal equity and post-flop difficulty against an aggressive Flash and short-stacked Minni. Standard fold spot.

I am in the Small Blind with Tc 6c. The Button has folded, leaving me heads-up against Minni in the Big Blind. Minni is quite short-stacked with only about 20 big blinds (2,070 chips). While Tc 6c is a decent suited hand, raising here might invite a shove from a short stack, which I'd prefer not to call with Ten-high. By calling (completing the blind), I see a flop cheaply and keep the pot manageable while retaining the ability to outplay the opponent post-flop or fold if they shove pre-flop.

I'm in the big blind with K♠Q♥ and ~20.7BB. SB limped, leaving heads-up action — this is a clear spot to raise and isolate. Raising to 300 total (≈3x BB) applies pressure and buys fold equity against limp-calling ranges (small pairs, suited connectors, weak A/Kx) while still getting value from worse Kx/Ax and medium pairs if called. A min-raise is too small (gives good odds to draws) and shoving is unnecessary and often gets called by worse hands that punish us; 300 is a balanced, standard sizing here.

I have Tc 6c in the Small Blind. After my initial call to complete the blind, Minni (Big Blind) raised to 300. Minni is playing a short stack of about 20 big blinds (1,870 remaining). I need to call 200 more to see the flop in a pot that will be 600. The pot odds are 2:1, and my hand is suited with some connectivity. While I will be out of position, the price is reasonable to see a flop. If I hit a strong piece of the board or a flush draw, I can look to potentially felt the short-stacked opponent. If the flop misses me, I can easily fold to a continuation bet.

I have flopped top pair with a Ten, although my kicker (6) is weak. The board is somewhat coordinated with straight and flush draws. Since Minni was the pre-flop aggressor and is playing a short stack, I will check to see if they continue with a bet. Given the stack-to-pot ratio, I am likely committed to this hand if Minni chooses to put more chips in.

I was the preflop aggressor and hold K♠Q♥ on a 3♠ T♥ 8♥ flop — two overcards plus a single-heart backdoor (Q♥). Flash (SB) checked, showing weakness and giving me initiative. Against a SB cold-caller’s wide range (broadways, suited connectors, small pairs, some hearts and occasional Tx), a ~50% pot continuation bet is standard: it folds many unpaired hands and small pairs, extracts value from worse Kx/Qx and unmade draws, and keeps the pot manageable given my marginal showdown equity (~24% to pair by river). I’ll bet 300 to apply pressure; if he folds I take the pot, if he calls I’ll re-evaluate on the turn (bet again when I improve or when the turn is safe, check/control if it bricks), and if he check-raises large I’ll generally fold because I currently have no pair and only limited redraws.

I have flopped top pair with Ten-Six suited on a coordinated board (3s-Th-8h). Minni, the pre-flop aggressor, has a short stack of about 15 big blinds remaining. By calling the 300 bet, I am getting 3:1 odds to continue. Given the low stack-to-pot ratio, I am likely committed to this hand, but calling allows Minni to continue with bluffs or semi-bluffs on the turn, whereas raising might force out everything except hands that beat me (like AT, KT, or overpairs). I will call and re-evaluate on the turn, though I expect to be playing for the rest of Minni's stack.

I have top pair (Tens) with a weak kicker (Six). The board is somewhat coordinated, and the turn card (5s) is mostly a blank. Minni has a short stack (1570 remaining with a pot of 1200). Since Minni was the aggressor on the flop and pre-flop, I will check again to see if they continue their aggression. Given the low stack-to-pot ratio, if Minni bets or shoves, I am likely priced in to call with top pair against a range that includes draws and potential bluffs, though I must be wary of better Tens or overpairs.

I’m on the button (acting last) with K♠Q♥ on 3♠ T♥ 8♥ 5♠. After the flop c-bet (300) Flash called and then checked the turn — that looks like a calling/paired or floating range rather than a pure bluff. I only have two overcards and no realistic flush/straight now: only 3 Ks and 3 Qs (6 outs) to pair on the river (~13% equity). The pot is already 1,200 and I have 1,570 behind, so betting here risks bloating the pot with a hand that will rarely improve and that will be crushed by the many hands that already have a pair.

With position, the prudent line is to control the pot and realize my equity: check. If I hit a K or Q on the river I can build a small value bet; if I miss and face a sizable bet I’ll fold and preserve chips, while I can call small bets as a bluff-catch in favorable spots. If the opponent checks through the river I’ll re-evaluate then (bet small for thin value on safe rivers or take the free showdown).

I have top pair with a Ten, but my kicker is weak. After Minni checked back on the turn, it's possible she has a medium-strength hand or a missed draw. The river 7s completes some potential straights (like 96 or 64), although those are less likely given the pre-flop action. By checking, I allow Minni to bluff with her missed draws or check back with hands that have showdown value. Given the stack sizes and the pot, I'm comfortable checking to see what she does, as betting likely only gets called by hands that beat me.

I miss — K♠Q♥ on 3♠ 10♥ 8♥ 5♠ 7♠ is just K-high. Flash called my flop c‑bet and then checked turn and river, which reads like a calling/paired or cautious showdown range (pairs, weak tens, occasional floats) rather than a big river bluff. I only have 6 outs to pair (~12–13%) and the pot is already 1,200 with 1,570 behind; betting here would bloat the pot with very little equity and will often get called by better hands (or fold out only the very weakest hands). Optimal, low‑variance line is to control the pot and realize equity: check to take the free showdown. If Flash bets a small probe I can call as a bluff‑catcher; I’ll fold to a large river raise to preserve stack.

I am on the button with Jack-Six offsuit. While I have the opportunity to open the pot, Jack-Six is too weak to justify a raise, especially with Minni in the small blind holding a very short stack of approximately 15 big blinds. Short stacks are more likely to shove over a button open, and I don't want to commit chips with a sub-marginal hand. Folding is the standard and most profitable play here.

A♣J♣ is a strong heads-up hand and I have positional advantage postflop (small blind is the button heads-up). With only Claude in the big blind and no raise yet, I want to take the initiative rather than limp/call out of the blind. Raising to 300 (3x BB) gives me fold equity vs a wide defending range, keeps SPR reasonable for postflop play, and preserves enough of my 1,520 stack (~1,220 behind) to maneuver on later streets. I’ll avoid the bare minimum raise (200) which is too inviting; if Claude 3-bets big I’ll re-evaluate (likely fold to an over-aggressive shove, call smaller 3-bets depending on sizing and reads). If he calls, I’ll C-bet favorable flops and use my position to extract value or control the pot.

This is a clear fold. I'm holding 9s2s, which is one of the worst hands in poker (often referred to as "the Doyle Brunson hand" ironically, given his famous hand with it). Even though Minni is extremely short-stacked at 1270 chips and will likely be pushing soon, this hand has virtually no equity and shouldn't be played from the big blind out of position. Against a raise, 9-2o is a mandatory fold. Folding preserves my stack for better spots and is the mathematically correct play here.

I’m on the button with Th4d and a 1,670 stack (~16.7bb). The cost to see a flop is only 100 into a 150 pot — very cheap. Th4o is a weak, often-dominated hand, so I don’t want to build a bigger pot or face a 3-bet from deep stacks by raising. Folding would be too tight given the price and position. Plan: call the 100 to use my positional advantage and evaluate the flop; proceed only with a real piece of the board or a favorable read, fold to heavy aggression otherwise.

Folding 4-9 offsuit from the small blind. This is a weak, uncoordinated hand that plays poorly postflop, especially out of position against both Minni and Flash. With a healthy 26k stack, I can afford to be selective and wait for better spots. The big blind still has action, which could make this situation more costly if I call.

I am in the Big Blind with Jack-Six suited. Minni has limped from the button and Claude has folded from the Small Blind. Minni is currently playing a very short stack of approximately 15 big blinds. While I could raise to pressure her, checking to see a free flop with a hand that has decent post-flop playability is the most prudent and low-variance play in this situation.

I have J-high on an Ace-high board in a limped pot. Minni is playing a very short stack, and I have no real equity or draws here. Checking is the most logical play to see how she reacts or potentially see a free turn card.

Flop: A♥ 5♦ 4♥; my hand T♥4♦ — I’ve paired my 4 (weak, vulnerable bottom pair) and hold T♥ which gives me no real immediate draw (only runner-runner heart/backdoor possibilities). Flash checked the flop; betting here as a bluff is risky because an Ace or any decent pair will call, and I have almost no fold equity. With ~15.7bb effective I want to control the pot size and realize equity rather than inflate the pot with a marginal hand. Plan: check to keep the pot small, call only very small probe bets for price, fold to substantial aggression, and re-evaluate on the turn (continue only with improvement or a clear read).

In this limped pot, the board is Ah-5d-4h-Tc. I hold Jc-6c, which is just Jack-high with no draw. Minni, who is on the button and very short-stacked, checked back the flop. The turn Ten of Clubs doesn't help me, but it's another card that doesn't hit her likely range of low cards or weak pairs if she didn't bet the Ace on the flop. However, there's no reason to build a pot with zero equity. Checking is the best course of action to see if I can realize any showdown value or potentially bluff the river if the board develops favorably.

I hold T♥4♦ on A♥5♦4♥T♣ — that's two pair (Tens and Fours). It’s a reasonably strong hand heads-up but vulnerable to many hands that beat me (any A‑x, T‑x with better kicker, sets/ trips, or pocket Tens/5s/4s). Flash checked flop and checked the turn after I limped; that gives him a wide, often weak checking range (Kx/Qx/Tx/medium pairs/bluffs) but could also contain slow‑played Ax. With ~15.7bb effective I want to extract value from worse Tens and medium pairs while keeping the pot controlled in case he has an Ace or trips. I’ll bet a modest size to get called by worse hands (T‑x, 99–66, etc.) but avoid committing my short stack to a large raise. If he calls I’ll re‑evaluate the river; if he shows significant aggression (a sizable raise or shove) I’ll fold this vulnerable two‑pair to avoid putting the last of my chips in against a range that heavily dominates me.

In this limped pot, I am facing a bet of 150 from Minni on the turn. The board is Ah-5d-4h-Tc. I hold Jc-6c, which is just Jack-high with no pair and no draw. Minni is playing a very short stack (about 14 big blinds) and her bet after checking back the flop could represent a Ten, a small pair, or even a slow-played Ace. Since I have no equity and no realistic way to win the hand without a large bluff—which is risky against a short stack who may be pot-committed—the most sensible play is to fold and preserve my chips.

6-5 offsuit is a weak unpaired hand with poor playability postflop. Although I'm on the button (best position) and getting 2.5:1 pot odds to call 100 into 150, the hand lacks sufficient equity to warrant playing. Even though Minni is short-stacked at 1720, I should fold weak hands and wait for better opportunities. With 26k chips and 77 hands remaining, I can afford to be selective and exploit position with stronger holdings. This is a clear fold.

I am in the Small Blind with Ace-Four of Spades, a strong starting hand for blind-on-blind play. The Big Blind (Minni) is short-stacked with only 1,720 chips (approx. 17 BBs). I will put maximum pressure on her by raising. A raise to 300 is standard and sets up a situation where I can comfortably call if she decides to shove her remaining stack, as my hand has significant equity against a wide shoving range.

I’m in the big blind with 8♣5♥ facing a button/SB open to 300 (pot 400, 200 to call). I only have ~17bb effective, and 8‑5 offsuit is a weak, easily‑dominated hand with very poor postflop playability out of position. Calling requires ~33% equity vs the opener’s range but I won’t realize that equity enough OOP, and this hand is far too weak to 3‑bet or shove as a bluff. Preserve stack and wait for a better spot — fold.

I am on the button in a three-handed game. King-Seven offsuit is a standard opening hand in this position to put pressure on the blinds. Minni is quite short-stacked (under 17 BB), which means I should be cautious of a shove, but I will raise to 300 to attempt to take the pot now or play in position. If Minni shoves, I will have to evaluate her range, but for now, the standard play is to open.

I’m in the small blind with 9♦7♠ (offsuit) facing a button open to 300. Pot is 450 and a call costs 250 (I’d need ~35.7% equity to break even). I’m out of position versus the raiser and there’s still the big blind (Claude) to act and likely defend. 9‑7o has poor playability OOP, low implied odds, and isn’t a good 3‑bet bluff candidate here given my shallow ~16.7BB effective stack (a shove or real 3‑bet would be high variance and often dominated). Preserving my stack to pick better spots is the highest EV line long‑term, so I fold.

Q2o is too weak to defend the big blind out of position against an aggressive button raiser. With marginal equity and inferior position post-flop, folding preserves chips for better spots where I can leverage my stack advantage (25,905) against the shorter stack at the table.

I have 8♧4♠ — a miserable offsuit hand with very poor equity, especially multiway. I’m on the button with a 1,670 stack (~16.7 BB). The pot is only 150 and calling 100 is cheap, but calling makes a likely multiway pot against deep, capable players (Claude has shown aggression and Flash defends), putting me in difficult postflop spots with a weak, often dominated hand. A min-raise to 200 could pick up the blinds sometimes but will also get called or 3-bet by players who can afford to apply pressure; I don’t want to bloat the pot with this holding. With my stack size and this specific holding, the +EV play is to fold and wait for a better spot to exploit position or shove with a stronger stealing range.

Q4o is a weak hand lacking sufficient equity to defend from the small blind, especially out of position against an aggressive opponent. While heads-up play typically allows for wider ranges, this hand is too marginal and difficult to play profitably post-flop. Folding preserves chips for better opportunities.

2-3 of hearts is the weakest hand in poker. Although I have position and my opponent (Minni) is extremely short-stacked at 1,570 chips, there's no justification for calling 100 with such a weak hand. I should fold and wait for better spots to apply pressure when I have a playable hand. With my healthy 25k stack, I can afford to be selective.

I have Ace-Six of diamonds in the small blind. The button has folded, so it is just me and Minni. Minni is extremely short-stacked with only 1570 chips (less than 16 big blinds). My hand is strong enough to play for stacks against a short stack in a blind-vs-blind situation. Raising is the standard play here to put pressure on the big blind or simply take down the pot. Given her stack size, I will raise to 300 to induce a shove or take the pot now.

Facing a 300 open from the SB heads-up with Tc3c. Pot is 400 and it costs me 200 to call (pot ~600 after call) so I’m getting ~33% pot odds. Against a SB open in a 3-handed pot his range is fairly wide, and T3s has reasonable equity (suit + backdoors and some two‑way showdown potential). My effective stack is only ~15–16bb, so a small 3‑bet or shove with this weak, often‑dominated holding is unattractive — I don’t want to commit a big portion of my stack as a pure bluff. Calling preserves fold equity post‑flop, keeps the pot controlled, and lets me continue on club draws or a ten; on ace/king heavy boards I can fold to large aggression. I’ll call the 200 (300 total committed: 100 BB already + 200).

I have flopped top pair with an Ace on a board of Ac-Td-7c. My kicker (6d) is weak, but against a short-stacked opponent like Minni (who has only 1370 chips remaining), top pair is a very strong hand. The board has a club flush draw and several straight draws (J-Q, 8-9). I want to bet here to get value from weaker Aces, tens, or draws, and to protect my hand. Given the pot is 600 and Minni's stack is quite small, a bet of 300 is roughly half the pot and sets up an easy commitment for the rest of her chips on later streets or if she raises.

Call. I have top pair (T♣) with a very weak kicker (3♣) but also a four‑card club draw (9 outs). Flash’s bet is a small c‑bet (300 into ~900) — calling 300 to win ~1200 needs only ~25% equity. With pair + a club draw I clear that threshold and still have showdown value against many of his wide SB raising/c‑betting combos. My stack (~1,370) is short (~13–14bb), so a min‑raise to 600 (nearly half my stack) is too committal here and will often be called by Ax or other hands that dominate my kicker. Folding is too tight given my equity. Plan: call to see the turn; if I complete the flush or make two‑pair/trips I’ll get aggressive, but I’ll fold to heavy action on an Ace or other dangerous card if I don’t improve.

I have top pair of Aces with a 6 kicker. The turn 2d is essentially a blank that doesn't change the board texture significantly. Minni is very short-stacked with only 1070 chips remaining and a pot of 1200. I want to continue betting for value against her likely holdings such as a Ten, a Seven, or a flush draw. A bet of 400 is around one-third of the pot, which is a standard size to keep her marginal hands in while setting up a potential all-in on the river.

I call. I have top pair (T♣) with a very weak kicker (3♣) plus a four‑card club draw (9 club outs). The turn bet is 400 into a 1,200 pre‑turn pot (current pot 1,600); calling 400 will make the pot 2,000 so I only need ~20% equity to break even. On the river I have ~14 clean outs (9 clubs + 2 tens + 3 threes) → 14/46 ≈ 30.4% to improve, which comfortably beats the required 20%, and I also have showdown value now. Raising/shoving here would commit too much with a weak kicker and will often be called by Ax/Kx hands that dominate me; folding is too tight given my equity. Plan: call to see the river; if I hit a flush/two‑pair/trips I’ll get aggressive, otherwise fold to heavy river aggression (especially on an Ace) or re‑evaluate based on the precise river card and opponent sizing.

I have top pair with an Ace on a board of Ac-Td-7c-2d-Ks. My opponent, Minni, is extremely short-stacked with only 670 chips remaining in a pot of 2000. Throughout the hand, she has been calling my bets, suggesting she has a marginal made hand like a Ten, a Seven, or perhaps a weaker Ace. The King on the river is a slight concern but unlikely to have improved her hand more than mine given the betting pattern. Since she is so short, I will move all-in to get the maximum value from her remaining chips, as she is priced in to call with almost any pair.

I have Tc3c on Ac Td 7c 2d Ks — top pair with a very weak kicker and I missed my club on the river. Flash has bet every street with small sizing; his river bet is 670 into a 2,000 pot (current pot shown 2,670). Calling 670 commits my remaining 670 (all‑in) and would make the final pot 3,340, so I only need ~20% equity to break even (670 / 3,340 ≈ 20.1%). Raw outs to improve are 14 (9 clubs + 2 tens + 3 threes = 14/46 ≈ 30.4%), and I also have showdown value with a pair of tens versus many bluffs and weaker pairs. Some outs are “dirty” (a ten can give an opponent A10 a full house; some club outs lose to higher club combinations), so my cleaned equity is lower than 30%, but still comfortably around the ~20% threshold. Folding here surrenders too much equity given my short stack; raising is not constructive (and I’m effectively capped). Given the pot odds, my showdown equity and the opponent’s wide/aggressive line, a call (all‑in) is +EV. I will call the 670.

Holding 6h 2c on the button, this is one of the weakest possible hands. While I am in position and the cost to call is only 50 chips, 62o has very little playability post-flop and doesn't merit an investment even at these odds. I'll fold and wait for a better spot.

Calling 50 chips to see the flop with 4♠ 9♠. In heads-up play with button position and a 2:1 chip advantage, this marginal hand is playable. Position will allow me to control post-flop action, and the 3:1 pot odds justify seeing the flop. I can fold to aggression if the board doesn't cooperate.

I have Ace-Nine offsuit (A9o) in the Big Blind. Claude, the only other player remaining in this 3-handed game (since Minni is not mentioned as active this hand), has completed the small blind (limped). A9o is a very strong hand heads-up or 3-handed, especially against a limping range from the Small Blind. I will raise to take the initiative and thin the field or win the pot immediately.

Calling 300 more (350 total invested) with 4♠ 9♠ in heads-up play. Strong pot odds (2.67:1), excellent position advantage, and the suited connector has good playability against Flash's wide raising range. Stack advantage allows me to leverage position post-flop. This is a standard call.

In this heads-up situation, I am the Big Blind and the pre-flop aggressor after raising Claude's limp. On a low, connected flop of 4c-6d-3c, the board texture somewhat favors a limp-calling range which often contains many small pairs and suited connectors. However, as the pre-flop raiser, I still maintain a range advantage with all the high pairs and strong broadway hands in my range. Holding Ace-Nine offsuit, I have two overcards and some backdoor equity. I will lead with a continuation bet of 300 (37.5% of the pot) to take the initiative, protect my hand against his high-card air, and potentially fold out hands with similar or slightly better equity like small pocket pairs that might feel vulnerable.

Calling 300 on the flop with 4♠9♠ and top pair. Receiving 4.67:1 pot odds with a dry board where my pair is likely ahead of Flash's range. Strong position advantage, chip lead, and small bet sizing favor calling to see the turn while maintaining flexibility. Top pair is solid on this texture.

The board is very low and connected (4-6-3-2), and the turn 2h completes a straight for any hand containing a 5, which is well within Claude's limp-calling range. Since I only have Ace-high and no real draws, continuing to bet into a board that favors the caller's range is risky. Checking here allows me to control the size of the pot and see what Claude does.

Betting 600 on the turn with top pair (4s with 9 kicker) and a flush draw. Flash's check indicates weakness after raising pre-flop and on the flop. The dry board, his passive turn action, and my position/chip lead all support a value bet here. This extracts value, builds the pot with strong equity, and tests Flash's holding while maintaining my advantage.

The board texture (4c-6d-3c-2h) is extremely unfavorable for my hand. As the pre-flop aggressor with Ace-high, I am now facing a significant bet on a turn that completes a straight for any hand containing a 5, which is well within Claude's limp-calling range from the small blind. While I have Ace-high, there are too many combinations in my opponent's range that have either made a straight or at least a pair. Calling here would likely lead to a difficult decision on the river without a clear path to winning the pot. I will fold and wait for a more advantageous situation.

I have been dealt a pocket pair of nines (9d 9c) on the button. This is a very strong hand in heads-up play. My goal is to raise to build the pot and gain initiative. A standard raise of 3x the big blind is appropriate here to narrow the field (though it's already heads-up) and extract value from weaker hands that Claude might call with.

Calling 200 to see the flop heads-up with ace-high. Flash's button raise range is wide, and A2o has decent equity against that range. Pot odds are favorable at 2:1. The call preserves my chips while allowing me to compete in a decent spot.

Checking with A2 of hearts on 6h-Jh-7c flop. Currently holding ace high but with a premium draw (nut flush draw + wheel straight draw, roughly 15 combined outs). Flash's button raise range is extremely wide in heads-up play. By checking, I gather information about Flash's hand strength while preserving my chips and controlling pot size with my strong drawing hand. If Flash bets, I have excellent equity to call. This is superior to betting with a currently weak made hand.

I hold a pocket pair of nines (9d 9c). The flop is 6h-Jh-7c. This is a relatively coordinated board but my hand is an overpair to two of the cards (6 and 7) and only one overcard (the Jack) is present. In a heads-up pot where I was the pre-flop aggressor, I should continue with a bet to extract value from straight draws (like 89, 58), heart draws, and middle pairs (like 6x or 7x) that Claude might check-call with. A bet of around half the pot is standard here to maintain pressure and define where I stand.

I'm holding Ah2h on a 6h-Jh-7c flop, which gives me an extremely strong draw: a nut flush draw (8 remaining hearts) and a wheel straight draw (any 4 completes it). This is approximately 11-12 total outs (roughly 35-40% equity), which is excellent equity against Flash's wide button-raising range. The pot is 900 and I need to call 300, giving me 3:1 pot odds. This is a profitable call with significant equity. Additionally, calling preserves my larger stack advantage against my shorter-stacked opponent while allowing me to realize my draw on the turn and river. Raising would be less efficient here—I'm either ahead or essentially flipping, making call the optimal line to continue accumulating value.

I'm holding Ah2h with a premium draw on the turn (board: 6h-Jh-7c-4s). I have approximately 16 clean outs: 10 hearts for the nut flush, 3 threes, and 3 fives for wheel/broadway straights. This gives me roughly 35% equity against Flash's wide button-raising range. With the pot at 1,200 and Flash checking the turn, this is an ideal semi-bluff spot. A 600 bet (about half pot) applies pressure to Flash's likely weaker holdings, builds the pot when I have strong equity, and potentially wins it immediately. If called, I'll hit my draw approximately 1 in 3 times on the river while maintaining my advantage in equity. Flash's check suggests weakness or a cautious hand, making this a profitable play to apply pressure and extract value.

I hold a pocket pair of nines (9d 9c). The board is 6h-Jh-7c-4s. I was the pre-flop and flop aggressor. On the turn, Claude leads out with a 'donk' bet of 600 into a 1200 pot. This lead often indicates a hand that has improved or a player who wants to set the price for a draw. While my nines are an overpair to most of the board, they are behind any Jack, two-pair, or sets. However, I am still ahead of his semi-bluffs (heart draws, straight draws like 58 or 8x) and potentially some stubborn middle pairs. Raising would likely only get called by hands that beat me, and folding is too weak given the pot odds and the strength of my hand. I will call to see a river and re-evaluate.

Checking on the river with Ace-high. My turn semi-bluff got called, which suggests Flash has a marginal hand—likely a weaker pair, ace with worse kicker, or a draw that didn't complete. The river brought 3h, which doesn't improve my hand (no flush with only 4 hearts, no straight). Flash's turn check indicated weakness. Betting here is risky: I either get check-called and lose chips with my weak hand, or my bet wins the pot but I don't get to see if Flash would have checked through. Checking controls pot size with a marginal hand and allows me to reach showdown cheaply. If Flash checks, I might win at showdown with Ace-high; if Flash bets, I can evaluate the situation then. This is the economical play with a weak made hand in position.

I hold pocket nines on a board of 6h-Jh-7c-4s-3h. I have showdown value as a middle pair. Claude led the turn with a donk bet and has now checked the river. While my nines beat his missed straight draws (like 89 or 8x) and smaller pairs (6x, 7x), there are many hands that beat me (any Jack, flushes, or straights like 58). Betting for value is thin because it is unlikely a worse hand calls, and I risk being check-raised by a stronger hand or a flush that is trapping. Checking back allows me to reach showdown and win the pot if my nines are the best hand.

In heads-up play, 7-9 offsuit is a reasonable call from the button. I'm getting 3:1 pot odds and can leverage my positional advantage post-flop. My larger stack allows me to outplay Flash in position. This hand can flop straights or pairs and has decent equity in a heads-up scenario.

I am in the Big Blind with 53o. Claude limped from the Small Blind. Given the weakness of my hand and the fact that I can see a flop for free, checking is the most sensible play to see if I can improve on the flop without committing any more chips.

I have bottom pair (threes) on a board with two overcards (King and Jack). Checking is the standard play here to see how the turn develops without inflating the pot with a marginal hand. Since Claude checked as well, I am happy to see a free turn card.

Continuation bet on the flop with positional advantage. Flash checked the K-J-3 board, signaling weakness. While 7-9 offsuit is complete air, taking down the pot immediately is far superior to going deeper with no equity. A 120-chip bet (60% of pot) is well-balanced sizing - enough to have fold equity while keeping losses manageable if called. Position and opponent weakness demand aggression here.

I have bottom pair with a very weak kicker (5) on a board featuring two overcards (King and Jack) and a flush draw. Claude limped from the Small Blind and is now leading for 60% of the pot on the flop. While I am getting decent odds to call, my hand has very little potential to improve to a winning position, and I am likely already behind any King, Jack, or better pair. To preserve my stack for better opportunities, I will fold.

In this heads-up situation, 9-6 offsuit is a relatively weak hand. While many players open a wide range from the button, I prefer to avoid unnecessary risks with marginal hands given Claude's significant chip lead. Folding allows me to wait for a better spot to commit my stack.

In heads-up play on the button with J-6o, calling is the optimal approach here. The hand has marginal value - it's too weak to raise for value against a short-stacked opponent, but it has enough equity to warrant seeing a flop from the small blind position. My significant chip lead (27,905 vs 11,945) means I can afford to play more hands profitably in position. J-6o plays reasonably well post-flop in heads-up where any pair or high card has value. Calling keeps the pot manageable and allows me to realize equity on the flop while maintaining my positional advantage throughout the hand.

I am in the Big Blind with Jd 8s. Claude, the Small Blind, has limped in. J8o is a mediocre hand, and while I could raise to try and take the pot immediately, Claude has shown a willingness to call pre-flop raises and play aggressively post-flop in limped or raised pots. Checking allows me to see a flop for free and keeps the pot small while I am out of position.

I have top pair (Jacks) with an 8 kicker on a board of 4d-Jh-Td. In this limped pot, I am facing a check from Claude (SB). While my kicker is relatively weak, top pair is a strong hand in a blind-vs-blind limped pot. The board is quite wet with straight and flush draws, so I will bet to get value from worse hands like Ten-x, pocket pairs, or diamond draws, and to protect my hand against overcards.

I'm raising to 350 total with top pair (Jacks with a 6 kicker) on a 4♦J♥T♦ flop. This raises Flash's bet from 100 to 250 more, building a pot of approximately 650 while maintaining control of the hand. Top pair is strong in heads-up play, and my chip lead allows me to apply profitable pressure. This raise extracts value from weaker pairs and draws while keeping Flash's range undefined. The aggressive line is optimal given my hand strength and positional advantage.

In a limped pot between the blinds, top pair is a very strong hand. I led for a small amount to get value from draws and middle pairs, and Claude raised to 350. While the board is draw-heavy (diamonds and straight draws) and my kicker is weak, folding top pair here would be too exploitable. Claude's range for raising could include flush draws, straight draws, or weaker Jacks. I will call the raise to see a turn card and evaluate the strength of my hand further, as raising would likely only get called by hands that beat me.

On the turn, the 9h has arrived, giving me an open-ended straight draw (any Queen or 7 completes it) to go along with my top pair. Since I am out of position and Claude raised me on the flop, checking is the most prudent play. It allows me to see how Claude reacts to this card. If he bets, I have significant equity with top pair and a straight draw to call. Checking also keeps the pot manageable while I am unsure if my top pair is still the best hand, given the board is becoming very connected.

I hold J♠6♥ giving me top pair (Jacks with a weak six kicker) on a board of 4♦J♥T♦9♥. Flash checked the turn after calling my aggressive flop raise, which indicates weakness. In heads-up play, this is a clear opportunity to extract value.

The board is moderately drawy (two hearts), but Flash's check action is a strong signal they don't have a strong made hand or are giving up on draws. With my significant chip lead (27.5k vs 11.6k), I should be exploiting this weakness.

A moderate value bet around 300 chips accomplishes several goals:
1. Extracts value from weaker pairs and marginal hands
2. Applies pressure to draws while not overcommitting
3. Keeps Flash's range undefined heading into the river
4. Maintains positional and chip lead advantage

This sizing is neither too large (which would fold out the marginal hands I'm trying to value bet) nor too small (which wouldn't properly build the pot). My top pair with a weak kicker is in a good spot to get value here.

On the turn, the 9h completes a few potential straight draws (87) and gives me an open-ended straight draw (any 7 or Queen) to go with my top pair. Claude's bet of 300 into a 900-chip pot (1/3 pot) provides me with excellent odds to continue. Given I have top pair and substantial equity with my straight draw, calling is the most profitable play. Folding is too tight given the pot odds and my outs, while raising is unnecessary as it would likely only be called by hands that currently beat me or have significant equity against me.

The board has become extremely coordinated on the river (4d-Jh-Td-9h-8c), showing four cards to a straight (8-9-T-J). Any Queen or Seven now completes a straight. Although I have improved to two pair (Jacks and Eights), this is a relatively weak hand given the board texture. Claude's aggression on the flop (raising) and turn (betting) suggests he has a strong range, which could easily include hands like QJ, QT, or even a slow-played set that has now been outdrawn or a straight. Checking is the most prudent play to see if Claude will check back with a weaker hand or to decide whether to call a bet based on his sizing, though I am likely beat if he bets large.

Checking the river with J♠6♥ (top pair, weak kicker) on 4♦J♥T♦9♥8♣. Flash's check indicates weakness after an aggressive flop start and turn calls. My 6 kicker is too weak to value bet profitably—if Flash calls, they likely have a better jack; if they fold, they had air anyway and wouldn't have called a river bet. Checking allows me to safely take this to showdown and win the pot against complete air while avoiding losses to superior jacks. This is the optimal play given my marginal hand strength and Flash's weakness indicators.

83o is a extremely weak hand, even in a heads-up scenario. While being on the button provides a positional advantage, the hand lacks any significant post-flop potential or raw equity. I will fold and wait for a more playable hand to engage.

Heads-up on the button with As9s is a clear raising hand. I have position and Flash is short-stacked (12645 vs my 27205). As9s has strong equity in heads-up play - it's a premium holding with ace-high and a decent kicker plus spade flush potential. Raising to 250 applies appropriate pressure while maintaining good fold equity and sizing. Flash has been fairly aggressive throughout the session, so I want to play hands in position and apply aggression myself.

I am in the Big Blind with 6c 2c. Claude, on the Button/Small Blind, has raised to 250. Although I am getting decent pot odds to call (150 to win a total pot of 500), 6c 2c is an extremely weak hand that performs poorly out of position. Given the stack depths and the fact that I'll be playing the rest of the hand out of position against an aggressive opponent, it's best to fold and wait for a stronger holding.

In this heads-up scenario from the small blind, I'm holding Qh3d. While Queen-high has some value, 3d is a very weak kicker. However, the cost to complete is only 50 chips into a 150 pot. Against Claude, who has shown a mix of aggression and willingness to check down boards, I can occasionally see a flop for cheap to try and hit a pair or a straight/flush draw. Given the high stack depth, I will complete the small blind to see if I can improve on the flop, but I will be ready to fold if Claude shows significant post-flop aggression.

I'm holding pocket kings (Kc-Kd), the second-best starting hand in poker. With significant stack advantage (27255 vs 12545) and only one opponent, this is a strong value-raising situation. Flash called from the button with the small blind, suggesting a wide, weak range. Raising to 300 (3x the big blind) accomplishes several goals: (1) builds the pot when I have a premium hand, (2) extracts value from weaker holdings, (3) applies pressure to Flash, and (4) defines the hand's strength early. This is a standard aggressive play that's profitable against Flash's likely weak button-calling range.

I am in the small blind with Qh3d. I initially called the big blind, but Claude has responded with a raise to 300. Q3 offsuit is a very weak hand that performs poorly against a raising range, especially out of position. Even though I am getting decent odds to call 200 more into a 400 pot, my hand is easily dominated and lacks post-flop playability. Given the stack depths and the fact that Claude has shown controlled aggression in previous hands, it is better to fold now and wait for a more favorable spot rather than bleed chips with a subpar hand.

Raising to 200 on the button in heads-up play against a short-stacked opponent with significant chip advantage. K-2 is weak but heads-up equity is much higher than in full games. This raise applies pressure, wins by default a good portion of time, and maintains positional advantage. Standard button aggression with the chip/position lead.

I have Ace-Jack of spades in the Big Blind. This is a very strong starting hand, especially in a heads-up situation. Claude, the Button (SB), has raised to 200. I have a stack of 12,445 and Claude has 27,255. Given the strength of my hand and the fact that I'm out of position, a 3-bet is the standard play here to build the pot and take the initiative. I will raise to 600 total.

Calling Flash's 3-bet with K-2o in heads-up. Getting 3:1 pot odds (only need 25% equity), K-2 has ~30-40% equity heads-up against wide ranges, significant chip advantage (2.3:1), short-stacked opponent likely 3-betting wide, and good position to act post-flop. Reasonable risk/reward at these odds and stack sizes.

On this flop of Td 9s 4s, I have two overcards and the nut flush draw. This is a very strong semi-bluffing board for me as the pre-flop 3-bettor. I have a lot of equity against Claude's range, which likely includes many pairs and some draws. To maintain the initiative and potentially win the pot immediately or build it for when I hit my flush or overcards, a continuation bet is standard. I'll bet about half the pot to keep the pressure on.

Calling Flash's flop bet of 600 into 1800. K2o receives 3:1 pot odds and needs only 25% equity. Against Flash's wide heads-up range, K-high with backdoor flush and top pair outs has approximately 28-35% equity. The marginal positive EV, combined with positional advantage on future streets and heads-up dynamics where Flash is likely betting wide, justifies the call. With a 2.4:1 chip lead, slightly marginal spots are profitable long-term.

I have turned the nut flush with the Ace of Spades. Having been the aggressor pre-flop and on the flop, I must continue to build the pot for value. The board now has four spades, which might be a bit scary for my opponent, but I want to get paid by any hands Claude might be calling with, such as a lower flush, a straight, or even a set. I will bet 1600 into the 2400 pot to extract value while leaving room for a significant bet or shove on the river.

Mathematically sound fold on the turn. K-2o has approximately 25.5% equity (12 outs) but needs 28.6% to break even with pot odds of 2.5:1. This represents -172 chips in expected value. Flash's aggressive play (preflop 3-bet, flop continuation, turn pot-sized bet into a 4-spade board) suggests a fairly strong hand. With 64 hands remaining in the series, taking marginal -EV spots is not optimal long-term strategy, even with chip advantage. Disciplined fold.
I am on the button with Queen-Jack offsuit, which is a very strong hand in a heads-up or short-handed situation. I will raise to 300 to take control of the pot and build value early.

Q3o is too weak to defend from the big blind against Flash's button raise. Despite the 2:1 pot odds, the hand has poor equity post-flop, I'm out of position, and Q3 specifically has a weak kicker that plays poorly in multiway pots. This is a standard fold with marginal holdings.

Heads-up button play with J5. This hand is within my opening range from the button given the wide range required in heads-up poker. I have positional advantage and a significant chip lead (26k vs 13.7k). A standard 3x raise to 300 total establishes aggression, allows me to win the pot immediately or play postflop in position where I can leverage my chip lead and positional advantage against Flash's aggressive tendencies.

I am in the Big Blind with 83s, a very weak hand. Claude has raised to 3x from the Button. Given the strength of my hand and the fact that I am out of position, there is no reason to defend or bluff here. Folding is the standard and most profitable play.

In a heads-up situation as the small blind with 83o, the hand is quite weak. While I have the button, 83o does not play well post-flop and doesn't have much equity against a standard big blind defending range. Given the high stack depths and the nature of the hand, folding is the most prudent play to wait for a better spot.

Heads-up on button with Q6s. This is a reasonable raising hand that plays well post-flop with positional advantage. Raising to 250 (2.5x BB) applies pressure, generates fold equity, and maximizes value with my chip lead. Calling is passive; folding would be weak in heads-up play with this equity.

In this heads-up situation, K6o is a standard defending hand in the Big Blind against a 2.5x raise from the button. I have sufficient pot odds (3.3:1) to justify a call and see a flop, given that my hand has reasonable high-card value against a wide button opening range.

In this heads-up pot, I am out of position as the Big Blind. I have King-high on a board of Ts-7s-4h, which does not connect with my hand. The standard play in this situation is to check to the pre-flop aggressor to see how they react to the board texture. King-high retains some showdown value against missed draws, so checking is the most prudent move.

Checking is optimal here. While Flash's check shows weakness and position would normally dictate a bet for fold equity, Q6 is a marginal hand and I have a significant chip lead (2:1). By checking, I: (1) preserve position for future streets, (2) control pot size with a weak hand, (3) allow Flash opportunities to over-extend with inferior holdings due to short-stack pressure, and (4) minimize risk while keeping my equity intact. The gutshot and backdoor flush draw provide outs if called on future streets. Given heads-up dynamics and my dominant chip position, this measured approach is superior to aggression with a marginal hand.

On the turn, the board is Ts-7s-4h-8d. I have King-high with a gutter-shot to a straight (though a 9 is needed for a higher straight and a 5 for a lower one, my 6 makes a straight if a 5 comes, but 9 is more likely to be in an opponent's range). Since Claude checked back on the flop, he likely has a marginal hand or is giving up. However, King-high has some showdown value and checking allows me to potentially see a free river or evaluate a small bet from the opponent without overcommitting with a weak hand.

On the turn with Q6h on Ts7s4h8d, I'm in position with my chip lead intact. Flash has now checked twice (flop and turn), indicating weakness or uncertainty in his hand. While I have legitimate outs (approximately 13 with any 9 for a straight or additional hearts for a flush draw), Q-high is marginal. By checking: (1) I reach the river in position, maintaining my informational advantage on the final street; (2) I keep the pot small with a hand that's far from nuts; (3) I allow Flash to potentially make a bluff attempt on the river, which I can exploit with my chip lead - if Flash bets, I can fold the worst hands, and my position lets me make an informed decision; (4) I avoid building a large pot with a marginal holding. This measured approach aligns with using my dominant chip position to induce mistakes rather than forcing marginal situations. The river will provide clarity and opportunity to apply pressure or fold appropriately.

On the river, the King of Spades gives me top pair. Claude has checked both the flop and the turn after raising pre-flop, which strongly suggests a capped or weak range. While the King of Spades completes a potential flush, Claude's passivity on earlier streets makes a flush unlikely. I will lead with a value bet of 300 (60% of the pot) to get called by Ace-high hands or small pairs that he might have been checking down.

Folding Q6 on the river. While Flash's check-check-bet pattern suggests possible weakness, my queen-high has minimal showdown value. With a 2:1 chip lead, I can afford to fold marginal situations and allow Flash's desperation to work against him through patient play. The risk of being wrong with the worst hand type outweighs the pot odds (need 27% equity, but likely have <25%). This disciplined fold maintains my advantage and applies implicit pressure through chip lead.

I have been dealt pocket tens, which is an extremely strong hand in a heads-up situation. My goal is to build the pot for value while also maintaining control of the hand. A standard opening raise to 300 chips is consistent with my previous play and provides a good balance between value and protection.

Calling with J7d in BB against button raise. Getting 2:1 pot odds with a connector that has flush draw potential. I have position post-flop which is valuable in heads-up play. Stack is deep enough to play post-flop decisions flexibly.

Betting top pair with a strong flush draw (15+ outs). The board 4c-Jc-5c is favorable for my Jd7d. Taking the initiative with a 60% pot-sized bet builds value while maintaining control post-flop. Deep stacks allow flexibility on future streets. This hand has sufficient equity and outs to warrant aggression here.

I have pocket tens on a flop of 4c-Jc-5c. I do not hold a club, which makes the board somewhat dangerous, but I still have an overpair to two of the cards. Claude led out with a bet of 350 into a 600 pot. This 'donk bet' could represent a variety of hands, including a Jack, a flush draw, or a smaller pair. Raising here allows me to define where I stand; if Claude reraises, I can be reasonably certain I'm against a flush or a set. If he calls, I can evaluate the turn. I will raise to 850 to put pressure on his draws and see how he reacts.

Calling the flop re-raise with Jd7d. Top pair plus an 11-out flush draw provides sufficient equity (40-50%) against Flash's re-raising range. Pot odds are favorable (4:1), and deep stacks allow flexible post-flop play. Calling maintains position and keeps the hand alive while gathering information about Flash's holding on the turn.

Checking the turn with Jd7d (top pair + 11-out flush draw) on a four-club board. Flash showed aggression on the flop, so checking allows me to: (1) control pot size with a strong drawing hand, (2) gather information about Flash's hand strength, (3) balance my range appropriately, (4) potentially see a free river if Flash checks back, and (5) avoid getting caught in another re-raise. Deep stacks allow flexible play, and I have sufficient equity to reach showdown. Checking is the prudent choice here.

The turn card is the Queen of Clubs, completing a four-flush on the board. Since I do not hold a club, my pocket tens have significantly decreased in value. Claude checked, which could indicate a made flush (trapping) or a hand without a club that is ready to give up. Betting here offers little value, as I am unlikely to get called by worse hands and will almost certainly be called or raised by any hand containing a single club. Checking back allows me to control the size of the pot and attempt to reach showdown cheaply or evaluate Claude's action on the river.

Checking the river with J♦7♦ (pair of Jacks) on a highly coordinated board (4♣ J♣ 5♣ Q♣ K♦). Flash's turn check indicates weakness—if they held KK, KQ, QQ, or QJ, they would likely have continued betting the turn. The river K is mostly a blank for Flash's most likely holdings (busted draws, weaker pairs, medium strength hands). By checking, I control the pot, see Flash's river action, and avoid unnecessary risk. If Flash bets, I can reassess based on bet sizing. If Flash checks, I reach showdown with a decent hand. This cautious line respects Flash's perceived weakness while protecting against being exploited by stronger holdings.

The river is the King of Diamonds, and the board now contains four clubs. I do not hold a club, which means any single club in Claude's hand beats me. Claude has checked for the second street in a row. If I bet, I will only be called by a club, and any hand I currently beat (like a smaller pair without a club) will fold. There is no value in betting. Checking back allows me to reach showdown and hope that Claude also does not have a club and that my pocket tens are the best non-flush hand, although even then I am losing to any Jack, Queen, or King.

Raising to 250 total (adding 200 to my 50 SB). In heads-up play from the button, I should be raising a wide range including Q-6o. Position and my 2:1 chip advantage make this a straightforward raise. This is a standard aggression play that allows me to win the pot immediately or proceed to the flop with positional advantage and superior stack depth.

I am in the Big Blind with Kc 9s. Claude, in the Small Blind/Button, has raised to 250. K9o is a standard defending hand in a heads-up or late position battle, especially given my stack size and the pot odds (calling 150 to win a total pot of 400). Claude has been active and aggressive, but K9o is strong enough to call a 2.5x raise pre-flop to see a flop. I will call the additional 150.

On the flop of 6c-Td-Qd, I have two overcards and a backdoor straight draw. Claude raised pre-flop, and this board connects well with a lot of his range (Qx, Tx, diamonds). Since I am out of position and have no made hand yet, checking is the standard play to see how Claude reacts. I want to keep the pot small while looking for an improvement on the turn.

I'm in heads-up play with a significant chip lead (2.15:1 advantage). I raised preflop with Q6o from the button and Flash called from the big blind.

On the flop (6c Td Qd), I've flopped top pair with a 6 kicker. Flash checked, indicating weakness. This is a strong opportunity to apply pressure.

Key considerations:
1. Top pair is likely the strongest hand in this spot
2. Flash's check signals weakness/miss
3. My position and chip stack allow me to play aggressively
4. A continuation bet is standard and profitable here
5. Pot is 500; betting ~350 (roughly 70% of pot) maintains balance between value and pot commitment

I'll make a continuation bet to win the pot and build the pot when ahead with my superior hand.

On a board of 6c-Td-Qd, my Kc 9s has very little equity. Claude's 70% pot-sized continuation bet (350 into 500) is strong on a board that favors his pre-flop raising range (which includes many Qx, Tx, and diamond draws). I only have a gutshot straight draw to the King-high straight (requiring a Jack) and one overcard (the King), which might not even be clean if he holds KQ. Being out of position with a marginal holding against an aggressive opponent, the most prudent move is to fold and wait for a better spot.

In Hand #93, I am on the Button (Small Blind) with Queen of Spades and Five of Diamonds (Q5o). The pot is 150 and it costs me 50 to call, but as a professional player, I recognize Q5o is a relatively weak hand even for heads-up play. However, Claude has been playing fairly straightforwardly. Given I am getting 3:1 odds to see a flop, I will call to keep the pot small and see if I can connect or outplay post-flop, rather than folding outright or making a loose raise.

With 2-6 offsuit (worst hand in poker), the correct play is to check and see the flop. In heads-up play, this weak hand becomes more playable, especially when I can see it cheaply. Flash's call of the small blind suggests a weak range. Post-flop position advantage combined with my chip lead allows me to win this pot on the flop if Flash shows weakness. Raising with 2-6 would be unprofitable even in heads-up situations.

I hold 2h-6d with a flop of 3s-9d-2s. While I have a pair of deuces, I'm in heads-up play against Flash who just checked. With the worst starting hand in poker, I need to be cautious about building a large pot. My chip lead (27355 vs 12445) and position allow me to control the hand postflop. Checking back keeps the pot manageable while maintaining optionality on the turn. Flash's check suggests weakness, so I can reassess once more cards come out. If Flash bets on the turn, I have clear decision points with more information. Checking is the prudent approach with a marginal hand when ahead in chips.

In this limped pot, the flop of 3s-9d-2s is relatively dry, and I have Queen-high with a backdoor spade draw. Claude checked to me, and while I could attempt a continuation bet, checking back allows me to control the pot size and realize my equity for free. My hand has some showdown value against his absolute air, and I don't want to turn it into a bluff immediately in a small pot where he might have a piece of the board or a draw.

I hold 2h-6d with a board of 3s-9d-2s-Ac. I have a pair of deuces, which is a very weak holding. The Ace on the turn is concerning - it's a card that completes many hands Flash could hold (A-K, A-Q, A-J, A-T, etc.). 

On the flop, Flash checked after I checked, suggesting weakness or a draw. However, the Ace significantly changes the dynamic. My pair of deuces is likely trailing to any ace Flash might hold.

With a massive chip advantage (27355 vs 12445), I can afford to play cautiously and see the river inexpensively. Checking allows me to:
1. Avoid building the pot with a marginal hand
2. Control the pot size when ahead in chips
3. Keep the hand simple - if Flash bets, I can fold; if Flash checks, I maintain optionality

Betting or raising with 2-6 would be speculatively aggressive with a very weak hand, especially after an Ace hit. Checking is the mathematically sound play here.

The turn brings an Ace, which is a significant scare card in a limped pot. Claude has checked for the second time, indicating he likely hasn't connected with the board or the Ace. By betting here, I can represent the Ace and take down the pot immediately. Given the small pot size and Claude's tendency to fold to post-flop pressure when he lacks a strong hand, a half-pot bet is a cost-effective way to win the hand without needing to see a river.

I'm holding 2h-6d, which has turned into a very weak hand. While I do have a pair of deuces, it's essentially the bottom pair on this board. The Ace on the turn is a significant development that likely improves Flash's hand considerably.

Flash's betting pattern (flop check followed by turn aggression after the Ace) suggests he either improved to a legitimate hand or is representing an Ace. My pair of deuces is vulnerable to numerous holdings in Flash's range, including any ace, overcards, or medium pairs.

While pot odds of 3:1 might suggest a call mathematically, my actual equity against Flash's likely range is probably well below the 25% needed to break even. The Ace is a dangerous card for my hand specifically. Additionally, I'm out of position for the river, which compounds the difficulty.

With a massive chip advantage (27355 vs 12345), I can afford to play tight and fold marginal hands. This is a textbook spot to fold a weak holding and preserve my dominant position. There will be better opportunities to win chips against Flash.

In heads-up play from the button with a significant chip advantage (27,305 vs 12,545), I should be playing a wide range aggressively. K-2o is a reasonable hand to raise with in position - it has decent showdown value with the king and represents a decent hand. My 2.2:1 chip lead allows me to apply pressure. A min-raise to 200 total chips puts Flash in a difficult spot where they must decide whether to defend their big blind or fold. This is standard aggressive button play in heads-up poker.

I am in the Big Blind with Ten-Five offsuit. Claude has raised to 200 from the Small Blind. Although I am getting 3:1 odds on a call, Ten-Five is a very weak hand that plays poorly out of position. Against a player who has shown consistent aggression and currently holds a significant chip lead, it is better to fold this hand and wait for a more favorable opportunity to engage.

I am on the button with 6c 2d. This is a very weak hand. While I'm only 50 chips away from calling the big blind, the hand's playability post-flop is extremely poor, and folding is the standard professional play to conserve chips for better opportunities.

I'm on the Button (Small Blind) heads-up against Flash with 3c Jc. In heads-up play, the button should be opening a wide range of hands, and J-3 is well within that range. My position advantage is significant - I act first pre-flop but last post-flop, which is valuable.

Key considerations:
1. Flash is short-stacked relative to me (12k vs 27k) - he may be playing more conservatively
2. Heads-up play requires aggression from the button
3. J-3 has reasonable playability - can hit top pair or trips
4. I have sufficient stack depth to apply pressure
5. Taking the initiative pre-flop with a raise is standard button strategy

A raise to 250 (2.5x BB) is standard and puts Flash to a decision while maintaining proper bet sizing. This allows me to control the pot, potentially win it pre-flop, and continue my aggression from good position.

In a heads-up situation, Jack-Seven offsuit is a standard defending hand from the big blind against a 2.5x raise. I am getting 3:1 odds on a call, which is sufficient to see a flop even though I will be out of position.

On this flop of 8d-6c-Qh, I have a gutshot straight draw (needing a 9) and one overcard to the middle of the board (Jack). Claude has the range advantage as the pre-flop raiser. I will check to see how he reacts, as leading out into the pre-flop aggressor on this board doesn't accomplish much. If he bets, I'll have to decide based on the sizing if it's worth continuing with my weak draw.

Betting 300 on the flop with my strong 4-flush draw and position advantage. Flash's check indicates weakness, and my hand has significant equity to improve. With Flash's short stack, applying pressure with a reasonable bet size allows me to win the pot immediately while maintaining proper risk/reward if called. The 3♣ J♣ has strong equity against most hands Flash could have called with pre-flop in heads-up play.

On a flop of Q-8-6, my J-7 offsuit has completely missed. While I am getting decent pot odds (300 to call into a pot of 800), I have no pair, no real straight draw, and no flush draw. Continuing with Jack-high here is unprofitable as I have very little equity and am out of position against the pre-flop aggressor. It is best to fold and wait for a better spot.

In a heads-up situation, 9-6 offsuit is a marginal hand. While one should play a wide range from the button, my opponent (Claude) currently has a significant chip lead and has been showing consistent pre-flop aggression.
