Given the stack depth and the weakness of this specific hand, I will choose to fold and wait for a better opportunity to commit chips against the larger stack.

In heads-up play on the button, I hold K♣3♠ with a significant chip advantage (27,755 vs Flash's 12,095). While K3 offsuit is marginal, it has decent equity in a heads-up situation. By raising from the button, I'm leveraging positional advantage and my chip lead to apply pressure on Flash in the big blind. Flash appears to be an aggressive player with a shorter stack, making him vulnerable to positional raises. A min raise to 200 is a standard aggressive play that either wins the blinds immediately or allows me to play a flop with position. This capitalizes on my chip advantage and positional strength.

I am in the Big Blind with 9h6s, facing a min-raise from Claude on the Button (SB). While 96o is a relatively weak hand, I only need to call 100 more into a pot of 300 (getting 3:1 odds). However, out of position against a player with a significant chip lead, it is often better to fold these weak, unsuited hands and wait for better spots rather than bloating pots with marginal holdings. I am in the Big Blind with A4s. Claude, the only other player remaining in the game, has raised to 300 from the Button (Small Blind). My hand is certainly strong enough to defend, especially in a heads-up situation. While a 3-bet is an option, calling allows me to control the pot size with a medium-strength Ace and see a flop. Claude has been active, and I have a healthy stack, though Claude currently holds the lead. Given the 2:1 odds on a call, I will call to see the flop.

On this flop of 10-4-2, I have middle pair with an Ace kicker. In a heads-up pot, this is a respectable hand, but it is not strong enough to lead out with a bet and build a massive pot. By checking, I allow Claude to continue with his wide button-opening range. I plan to check-call a reasonable bet to see if I can improve on the turn or get to a cheap showdown.

Flash checked a dry 2-4-T flop after calling my button raise with K7s. This is a standard continuation bet situation. Flash's check indicates weakness, and the board texture (three unpaired cards with limited connectivity) is unlikely to have helped their big blind range. With my substantial 2:1 chip advantage, I should apply pressure and take down this pot. A bet of 375 (roughly 60% of the 600 pot) is appropriately sized to represent strength while maintaining reasonable bet sizing.

I have middle pair (fours) with an Ace kicker on a 10-4-2 flop. In a heads-up pot, this is a relatively strong hand against a wide opening range from the button. Claude's continuation bet is standard, and I am not ready to fold a pair with the best possible kicker. I will call to see the turn and re-evaluate, keeping the pot manageable.

I have middle pair (fours) with an Ace kicker. On the turn, the Seven of Clubs is a relatively blank card that doesn't change the board texture significantly. Since I am out of position against an aggressive opponent who has the chip lead, I will check to maintain pot control. My hand is a decent bluff-catcher, but leading out would likely force folds from hands I beat and get called or raised by hands that have me beat. I will check and evaluate Claude's action.

Turn action with K7s that now has a pair of 7s. Flash has checked twice, showing weakness. The 7c on the turn significantly improves my hand to middle pair with a strong kicker. Given Flash's lack of aggression and my chip advantage, I should continue applying pressure with a proportional bet of 750 (roughly 55% of the 1350 pot). This maintains my aggressive line from the flop and likely takes down the pot, or gets value if Flash has a weaker hand. The turn card is highly favorable and Flash's passivity indicates this is the right moment to continue my aggression.

On the turn, the Seven of Clubs doesn't drastically change the board texture. I still hold middle pair (fours) with the best possible kicker. In a heads-up match against an aggressive opponent like Claude, who has a wide range from the small blind, this hand is too strong to fold to a single-street bet of roughly 35% of the pot. I am getting good odds to call and see a river. I will check-call to continue as a bluff-catcher, as raising would likely only get called by better hands (like a Ten or an overpair) and fold out his bluffs.

On the river, the board is Ten-Seven-Six-Four-Deuce. I hold a pair of fours with an Ace kicker. While this was a middle pair on the flop, it has become a relatively weak hand as the board has developed. However, in a heads-up match against an aggressive opponent like Claude, checking is the most prudent play. I have some showdown value against missed draws (like diamonds), but I am unlikely to be called by any worse hands if I bet. By checking, I can potentially see a cheap showdown or decide whether to call a final bet based on the sizing and Claude's perceived range. Given the pot size and the texture, leading out is not beneficial.

River decision with K7s that has improved to a pair of 7s (pair of 7s with King kicker on a 2-4-T-7-6 board).

Flash has checked three streets in succession - the flop, turn, and now the river. This repeated passive action strongly indicates weakness. Flash either:
1. Missed a draw (e.g., flush draw that failed to materialize)
2. Has a very weak hand and is giving up
3. Has an unpaired hand that can't beat my pair

My betting line throughout the hand (c-bet flop, aggressive bet turn, now river) has represented consistent strength and aggression. With my substantial chip advantage (25,730 vs 11,420), I should continue applying pressure.

My hand value is solid - a pair of 7s with a King kicker is a legitimate winning hand on this dry board runout. Flash's checking pattern, combined with the board texture (2-4-T-7-6 is a relatively weak board that likely missed Flash's big blind range for premium holdings), makes this an ideal spot to extract value.

I'll bet 1400, which is roughly half-pot on the river. This sizing:
- Represents strength consistent with my line
- Forces Flash to make a decision with their limited stack
- Gains value from weaker holdings Flash might call with
- Likely wins the pot outright given the checking pattern

This maintains my aggressive winning poker strategy and capitalizes on Flash's weakness.

On the river, the board is 10-7-6-4-2. My pair of fours, which was middle pair on the flop, is now fourth pair. Claude has bet three consecutive streets, including a near half-pot bet on the river. While the missed diamond draw and some straight draws (like 5-3 or 8-9) are in his range, triple-barreling usually represents a polarized range heavily weighted toward value, such as a Ten, a Seven, or an overpair. In a heads-up match, while it's important to defend, calling three streets with bottom pair against a consistent aggressor is likely a losing play in the long run. I will fold and wait for a better spot to commit more chips.

I am on the button with T8s, a strong suited connector that plays well in position. The small blind and big blind are the only players left. I have 113.7 big blinds and Claude has 284.8 big blinds. Raising is the standard play here to take control of the pot and put pressure on the big blind. A standard 3x open is 300.

Raising to 700 with pocket 77 from the big blind. Flash's button raise is likely quite wide given their aggressive tendencies throughout the session. With a medium pair, I want to build value and thin their range. My healthy stack and Flash's shorter stack make this raise appropriately sized - it puts real pressure on Flash (700 more to call) while building the pot with what's likely the strongest hand. This is a standard 3.5x raise that forces Flash to make a decision without over-committing myself.

I have T8s on the button (small blind) in a heads-up situation against Claude. I opened to 300, and Claude 3-bet to 700. T8s is a very strong hand for a wide button range and handles post-flop play well, especially in position. Calling the 3-bet is a profitable play given the odds (400 to call into a pot that will be 1400) and the deep stack depth (over 100bb). Folding would be too tight for this holding, and 4-betting is unnecessary as I want to realize my equity and leverage my position post-flop.

Continuation betting the flop with my 77 after a 3-bet pre-flop. The 6d-Qd-8c board is relatively dry and doesn't strongly favor most of Flash's button-raising range. While the Queen is present, my underpair is likely ahead of the majority of hands Flash would play from the button (small pairs, broadway cards without a Q, suited connectors). A 900 bet (about 64% pot) applies appropriate pressure on Flash's short 10,720 stack without over-committing. This is a standard continuation bet that maintains my aggression, gets value from worse pairs, charges draws, and capitalizes on my positional advantage as the pre-flop raiser.

On a board of Qd-8c-6d, I have middle pair (8s) with a ten kicker. Claude 3-bet pre-flop and is now leading for roughly 64% of the pot. While his range contains many hands that beat me (AQ, AA, KK, JJ, sets), it also contains overcards that missed (AK, AJ) and some semi-bluffs. With middle pair and position, I have a standard 'call' to see how the turn develops. I am getting decent pot odds and folding now would be too exploitable given I have a piece of the board.

Checking the turn. The Ace is a nightmare card for my pocket 77 - it heavily favors Flash's likely holdings from their button raise (AK, AQ, AT are all now strong). While I was the pre-flop aggressor, continuing to bet into the Ace when Flash is short-stacked and likely to have hit is not a good value play. Checking allows me to see the river affordably, gives Flash a chance to bluff, and avoids committing more chips with likely the weaker hand. This preserves my substantial stack advantage while remaining flexible on the river.

On this Turn, the Ace of Clubs is a significant scare card. Since Claude 3-bet pre-flop and led the flop, his range is heavily weighted toward high cards like AK, AQ, and big pairs. Claude's check on the turn could indicate that he has missed with hands like KK or JJ and is now cautious of the Ace, or he might be trapping with a strong hand. My middle pair (8s) has some showdown value but is unlikely to get better hands to fold if I bet. By checking back, I control the pot size, realize my equity, and can potentially see a showdown or evaluate a river bet from Claude more effectively.

Checking the river with 77 on a board of 6d Qd 8c Ac 5c. The Ace on the turn significantly weakened my hand, and Flash's turn check suggests weakness with their short 9,820 stack. By checking, I avoid losing additional chips with likely the weaker hand, while allowing Flash to bluff if they're weak or checking back if they have nothing. This preserves my substantial chip lead (26,980 vs 9,820) and gives me a decision point if Flash bets. The river blank (5c) doesn't improve my hand, making continuation too risky. Checking is the prudent play that balances value extraction with risk management.

On the river, the board is 6d-Qd-8c-Ac-5c. I hold middle pair (8s) with a ten kicker. Claude, who 3-bet pre-flop and led the flop, has now checked the turn and the river. His double check suggests he either has a medium-strength hand that is afraid of the Ace (like KK, JJ, or 99) or he has missed his draws (like diamonds). My pair of 8s has decent showdown value against his missed draws and lower pairs, but it is unlikely to fold out a better hand if I bet, nor will it likely get called by a worse hand. Checking back allows me to reach showdown for free and maximize the utility of my marginal made hand.

Raising K7s from the button in heads-up play. This hand has decent potential with king-high and a spade draw. I have position and a significant chip advantage over Flash who is short-stacked. A moderately aggressive raise applies pressure and positions me well for post-flop play. Flash will need to make a decision with a fairly wide range of hands from the big blind.

I am in the Big Blind with 4s6c. Claude has raised to 2.25x from the Button. While the pot odds to call are relatively good (125 to win a total pot of 450), 4s6c is a very weak, unsuited hand that performs poorly out of position. Given that there are still several hands left in the match and I want to avoid bleeding chips with marginal hands against an aggressive opponent who covers me, folding is the most prudent play.

I am on the button with K2 suited. This is a standard opening hand in a heads-up situation. Although my stack is smaller than Claude's, I still have plenty of big blinds to play. Raising here puts pressure on the big blind and allows me to take control of the pot early. A standard 3x raise is effective.

I'm on the Big Blind with A7o against Flash's button raise to 300. I have a significant chip lead (26,980 vs 12,620). A7o is a playable hand with decent equity against Flash's likely wide raising range from the button. I'm getting 2:1 odds to call (200 to win 400) and will have position post-flop. Flash has been somewhat aggressive but not overly tight in this game. The hand has good potential - top pair with Ace kicker, straight possibilities, and playability on many flop textures. Calling is the optimal play here rather than folding too tight or raising speculatively.

Checking with A7o on a dry 9-4-6 flop. This hand is vulnerable to many of Flash's likely holdings (pairs, broadway cards, draws). Playing OOP with a chip lead, I should gather information rather than bet from behind. Checking allows me to see Flash's action and control pot size. If Flash checks back, I get a free card. If Flash bets, I can reassess with more information about their hand strength.

On this flop of 9c-4c-6h, I have a flush draw with my King-high clubs (Kc 2c). Since Claude checked to me, this is a prime opportunity for a continuation bet. A bet here serves as a semi-bluff; I can win the pot immediately if Claude folds, but I also have significant equity to improve to a flush or potentially hit a King on later streets. I will bet 400, which is approximately two-thirds of the pot, to put pressure on his middle-card range.

Calling with A7o on 9c-4c-6h flop. Getting 3.5:1 pot odds (400 to win 1400). A7o has reasonable equity (~40-45%) against Flash's wide button range. While out of position, the pot odds and hand strength justify calling. With a significant chip lead (2:1), I can afford to see the turn and reassess. Flash's aggressive line is consistent with a c-bet on a dry flop, and Ace-high plays reasonably as a showdown value hand. The math supports calling and remaining flexible on the turn.

Checking with A7o on a dry 9-4-6-3 turn. This hand is vulnerable and the 3d is a blank. Out of position, I should gather information about Flash's holding rather than bloat the pot with marginal Ace-high. Checking allows me to reach the river efficiently, control the pot size with my chip lead, and evaluate Flash's turn action. If Flash checks back, I have showdown value. If Flash bets, I can make an informed decision based on their action pattern.

On the turn, the 3d is largely a blank card that doesn't change the board texture much. I still hold the nut flush draw with the King of clubs. Claude has checked to me again. While I could continue my aggression with a second barrel to try and force a fold from middle-pair hands (like a 6 or 4) or weak 9s, checking back here allows me to realize my equity for free. By checking, I keep the pot manageable and get to see the river card without risking more chips. If a club hits, I'll have the near-nut hand and can look to value bet. If I miss, I still have King-high which occasionally wins at showdown in heads-up play, or I can decide whether to bluff on the river based on the final card.

Checking with A7 on river after Flash checks twice. This is a showdown spot where I have marginal ace-high that will occasionally win. Betting here would be a pure bluff that's ineffective against Flash's checked-down line - they showed weakness by checking turn and river after c-betting the flop, indicating they likely don't have a strong hand. With a significant chip lead (26k vs 12k), checking safely to showdown is the optimal play. Flash's action pattern (raise + c-bet then passive) suggests they whiffed or have medium-strength at best, making A-high a reasonable showdown hand.

On the river, the board has paired with a second four. I missed my nut flush draw, but I still hold King-high. Claude has checked for a third time after calling my flop continuation bet. While I could attempt to bluff here to represent a nine or a four, King-high actually retains some showdown value in heads-up play, especially against missed straight draws like 8-7 or 7-5, or lower missed flush draws. Since Claude called the flop, he likely has a marginal piece of the board (like a six or a seven) that he might not fold to a single river bet. Checking back allows me to reach showdown for free and potentially win the pot without risking more chips on a bluff that might be called by a sticky opponent.

Heads-up button play with K4o. Raising is standard to apply positional pressure. Flash has shown aggressive tendencies, so I should be raising frequently from the button. A 2x raise (to 200 total) is appropriate to control the pot and win preflop or play in position post-flop with my chip advantage.

I am in the Big Blind with 6s 3d, which is an extremely weak hand. Claude has raised to 200 from the Small Blind/Button. While it is only 100 more to call, 63o is near the bottom of any range and plays very poorly out of position. Given the stage of the game and the goal to preserve my stack for better opportunities, there is no reason to defend such a weak hand.

In heads-up play, the button (small blind) should be played aggressively to capitalize on position. Although 8h 6c is a relatively weak starting hand, a standard 3x open is appropriate to apply pressure. If Claude 3-bets, the hand is weak enough to fold without significant loss, but if called, I will have the advantage of acting last on all subsequent streets.

Folding K-5o (King-Five offsuit) from the big blind against Flash's button raise. This is a weak hand that plays poorly out of position. Despite reasonable pot odds of 2:1, the hand's poor quality and positional disadvantage make this an easy fold. With a significant chip lead (27,780 vs 11,820), there's no need to defend marginal hands. Better spots will come.

I hold pocket fours (4s4c) on the button in heads-up play. With a 27730 stack versus Flash's 12120, I have a significant chip advantage. 

In heads-up no-limit hold'em, pocket pairs are solid holdings that warrant aggression, especially from the button. Pocket fours has:
- Showdown value
- Position advantage
- Ability to build the pot against Flash's weaker range
- Implied odds with my large stack

I should raise rather than call to:
1. Take the initiative and build the pot
2. Maintain positional advantage
3. Apply pressure given my chip lead
4. Extract value from Flash's weaker hands

A raise to 300 (3x the big blind) is standard aggression in this spot - not so large that I'm overcommitting with a medium pocket pair, but substantial enough to put meaningful pressure on the opposition.

I am in the Big Blind with J8o. The Button (Claude) has raised to 3BB (300). J8o is a borderline defending hand against a 3x open, but given the stack depths and the fact that we are heads-up, it is often a call to see a flop, especially since I'm getting 2-to-1 odds. I will call and evaluate the flop.

In this heads-up situation on the flop, I have an inside straight draw (needing a 9) and a Jack overcard to the board. As the big blind who called a pre-flop raise, the standard play is to check to the aggressor. The board (6-7-T) is fairly coordinated and could hit both ranges, but as the player out of position, I will check and see how Claude reacts before deciding whether to continue with my gutshot.

I'm holding 4s4c on a flop of 6c 7h Ts in heads-up play with a significant chip lead (27480 vs 11920). Flash checked, giving me the initiative.

Analysis:
- My pocket fours have showdown value but no direct hits on this board
- Flash's check suggests weakness or a cautious line
- In heads-up play with position and a chip lead, I should maintain aggression
- Checking back allows Flash to catch up cheaply or improve his hand
- Betting continues my narrative from pre-flop and applies pressure

Bet sizing:
- The pot is 600
- A standard continuation bet should be 2/3 to 3/4 pot
- I'll bet 400, which is roughly 2/3 pot
- This size maintains pressure without overcommitting with a marginal holding
- It's a reasonable bet that Flash needs to respect while not being so large that I'm pot-committed with a medium pocket pair

This is a straightforward continuation bet that maintains initiative and applies pressure given my chip advantage.

On the flop of 6-7-T, I have a gutshot straight draw (needing a 9 to make a 6-10 straight) and one overcard (the Jack). Claude has fired a continuation bet of 400 into a 600 pot (66% pot). The pot odds are approximately 2.5-to-1, meaning I need about 28% equity to continue profitably. A gutshot only hits about 16% of the time on the next card. While I have some implied odds if I hit the 9, the 2/3 pot sizing from Claude is quite steep for a non-nutted gutshot out of position. Given that we are nearing the end of the session, I will choose the conservative route and fold this marginal hand rather than chasing a draw against a significant bet.

I am on the button with 5c 6h, a weak speculative hand. In a heads-up situation, I can sometimes raise this to put pressure on the big blind, but given the current match context and stack sizes, I will stick to a standard play and fold this hand to maintain my stack for better spots.

5-3 offsuit is one of the worst starting hands in poker. While it only costs 50 more to call, this hand has minimal equity against Flash's big blind range and plays poorly postflop. With 11 hands remaining and a significant chip lead (28,080 vs 11,770), I should focus on playing premium hands and maintaining my stack. Folding weak hands from the small blind is standard strategy, especially when not in late-stage desperation.

With only 10 hands remaining and a significantly smaller stack than my opponent, I need to be selective about the pots I enter. Hand #141 deals me 5-2 offsuit on the button, which is statistically one of the weakest hands in the game. While the cost to call is only 50 chips, the post-flop playability is extremely poor, and I am unlikely to win the pot without significant improvement or a high-risk bluff. Given the current game state, it is more prudent to fold and wait for a better spot to utilize my chips.

I'm in heads-up play on the button with J6s, facing a 50 chip call. While J6s is marginally weak in isolation, several factors make calling correct here: (1) I have significant stack advantage (28k vs 11.7k), allowing me to play wider ranges profitably from position, (2) J6s is suited, giving additional equity through flush possibilities, (3) position advantage post-flop as the button allows me to control the pot and play small-ball poker, (4) in heads-up, my opening range should be significantly wider than in full games. I'll call to see the flop where I can leverage my positional and stack advantages.

I am in the Big Blind with K d 7 d. Claude, the Button (SB), has just completed the blind by calling the 100 total. This indicates a relatively wide or weak range from Claude. K7 suited is a strong heads-up hand that plays well post-flop, and I have the opportunity to take the initiative and build a pot against a capped range. I will raise to 300 to thin the field and put pressure on Claude's limping range.

In heads-up play, I'm on the button with J6d, facing Flash's raise to 300. My stack (28k) significantly outweighs Flash's (11.5k), giving me substantial positional leverage and flexibility post-flop.

J6 suited is a solid hand in heads-up for several reasons: (1) it has reasonable equity against most ranges, (2) it's a suited connector with two cards to flushes, (3) position as small blind/button is a major advantage, and (4) my stack advantage allows me to play wider profitably.

Flash's 2.5x raise from 100 to 300 is moderate and doesn't suggest an extremely premium range. Calling 200 more (250 total including my initial 50) allows me to see the flop in position where I can apply pressure, control the pot, and leverage my superior stack. This is a standard heads-up play rather than folding marginal equity or reraising J6.

I am the pre-flop aggressor after raising Claude's limp. The flop is dry with a Queen high, and I have King-high with a backdoor flush draw. I will continue with a standard continuation bet of slightly over half the pot to maintain my initiative and try to win the pot immediately against Claude's capped limp-calling range.

I'm in heads-up play on the button with J6d against Flash's 350 flop raise. The board is 6c Qd 2h, giving me a pair of 6s with a Jack kicker and a backdoor flush draw. Key factors supporting this call: (1) Pot odds are favorable (350 to call into 950 pot, getting approximately 2.7:1), (2) Significant stack advantage (27.8k vs 11.2k) allows me to play competitively and leverage position, (3) My pair of 6s is likely ahead of many hands in Flash's range, especially draws and smaller pairs, (4) Flash's 350 bet is moderate-sized (less than 0.5x pot), not indicating extreme strength, (5) Position advantage post-turn allows me to control the action, (6) Against Flash's range in heads-up play, J6 has solid equity. This is a standard heads-up situation where position and stack advantage make calling the profitable play rather than folding. I can reassess on the turn card.

On the Turn, an Ace of Spades appears, which is a significant scare card. Since I was the pre-flop aggressor and continued on the flop, this card hits my perceived range much harder than Claude's (who limped and called pre-flop). Claude's call on the flop likely indicates a Queen, a six, or a middle pocket pair. By betting here, I am putting maximum pressure on those 'one-pair' hands that now have to worry about a higher pair on the board. I will fire a second barrel of about two-thirds the pot to represent the Ace and try to take the pot down now.

Calling 850 on the turn with J6d against Flash's aggression. Board: 6c Qd 2h As. I have bottom pair with jack kicker and a backdoor flush draw. Despite the ace on the turn being concerning, I have adequate equity against Flash's likely range (roughly 29-35% including my draw). Pot odds of 2.5:1 justify the call. Key factors: (1) Stack advantage (27k vs 10.4k) allows me to weather uncertainty, (2) Position advantage post-river gives me control, (3) Flash's 850 bet is 68% of pot—not extreme strength, (4) My pair might still be ahead against many hands in his range, (5) I can reassess on the river based on his action or my hand strength. This is a standard heads-up continuation where pair + draw + position + stack size warrant seeing the river.

Claude has called a pre-flop raise, a flop continuation bet, and a turn barrel on an Ace-scare card. This sequence strongly suggests Claude has a made hand he is unwilling to part with, likely a Queen (Qx) or a small Ace. Since the river 8s is a blank that doesn't significantly alter the board's texture or complete any draws I could be bluffing with, a third barrel is unlikely to generate enough fold equity against a sticky opponent. I will check and prepare to fold to a bet, as King-high has no showdown value here.

River decision with J6d (bottom pair, weak kicker) on 6c-Qd-2h-As-8s board. Flash checked, indicating weakness. Key factors: (1) I have bottom pair with jack kicker, which is a marginal holding, (2) Flash's river check is a clear weakness signal after aggressive turn betting, (3) The 8s on the river doesn't improve my hand or alter the board texture significantly, (4) My hand strength (just bottom pair) is vulnerable to being beaten by any Queen, Ace, or even better 6x combinations that Flash might have, (5) Given Flash's turn aggression followed by river check, his range likely consists of missed draws or hands he's given up with, (6) If I bet, I face the risk of either getting called by better hands or folding out hands I'm currently beating. The prudent play is to check and take down the pot with my marginal holding. This avoids inflating the pot with weak equity and respects Flash's weakness signal. I should not try to extract additional value with J6 high in this spot - the risk of losing an additional pot outweighs the reward of potentially gaining chips.

I am holding 6c 2h on the button. This is one of the weakest starting hands in poker. While I am on the button in a heads-up situation, which often justifies a wide opening range, 62o is generally considered a standard fold. Given that we are nearing the end of the session (hand 143 of 150) and I have a comfortable stack, there is no need to play such a low-equity hand and risk chips against a deep-stacked opponent like Claude.

Raising to 250 with 9♥J♠ from button against short-stacked opponent. My 3:1 chip advantage and positional edge justify aggression with this decent hand. Standard 2.5x raise maintains initiative, applies pressure, and keeps Flash in range for potential post-flop value extraction.

I am in the big blind with T3s. Claude has raised to 250 from the small blind/button. T3s is a relatively weak hand, but it has some suited connectivity and playability. Given the stack depths and the fact that we are nearing the end of the session, I could defend, but T3s is at the very bottom of a defending range against a 2.5x open. However, in a heads-up or short-handed dynamic, I can occasionally call to see a flop. Looking at past hands, Claude has been fairly active. I'll choose to fold here to preserve my stack as I'm out of position with a marginal hand.

In this heads-up situation, I'm in the small blind with 9c7c, a solid suited connector. Since I'm on the button, I have a wide opening range. Claude is the big blind with a significantly larger stack. I'll open with a standard raise to 300 to build the pot and take control of the hand from a position of strength.

K4 offsuit is a marginal hand that doesn't warrant further investment from the big blind. While Flash has a short stack (9,920) and may be stealing, and I'm getting 2:1 odds on a 200 chip call, K4o is simply too weak to call against a button raise. King-high without kicker strength is a fold in this spot. With 6 hands remaining and a healthy chip lead, folding marginal hands and preserving equity is the correct long-term strategy. I'll conserve chips and wait for better situations.

I have 9h8h on the button with a significant chip lead (29630 vs 10220). This is Hand 146 of 150, so I'm in the late stages of the tournament. 9-8 suited is a reasonable hand to play from the button in position against a single opponent. The cost to call is minimal (only 50 chips relative to my stack), and my positional advantage will give me flexibility post-flop. With my chip lead, I can afford to play somewhat wider ranges and control the hand. I'll call to see the flop.

I am in the Big Blind with Ace-Deuce offsuit. Claude, the chip leader, has limped from the Button. While raising is an option to exert pressure, checking allows me to see a flop for free with a hand that has some high-card value but poor playability in large pots out of position. Given we are near the end of the session, I will take the free flop and evaluate.

On a Queen-high board, my Ace-high has some showdown value but is vulnerable. Claude checked back pre-flop after limping. I'll check to see a turn for free and see if I improve or if Claude shows weakness. Betting here doesn't achieve much as better hands call and worse hands fold.

Betting 150 on the flop with a pair of nines and an open-ended straight draw. Flash's check indicates weakness, and I should be aggressive with my strong hand and position. A 3/4 pot bet applies pressure while keeping the bet size reasonable relative to the pot. With my massive chip lead in the final hands, I can afford to play confidently. This bet should either win the pot now or get called by a worse hand.

I have Ace-high on a Queen-Seven-Nine rainbow board in a limped pot. Claude bet 150 into 200 after I checked. While Ace-high has some showdown value, this texture hits a wide limping range well (many 7x, 9x, and some Qx). However, against a single 3/4 pot bet in a heads-up limped pot, Ace-high is often still competitive or has enough equity against bluffs to continue for one street. If I fold now, I give up the pot too easily. I will call and re-evaluate on the turn.

On the turn, the King of Diamonds is an overcard to the flop's Queen. Having checked and called on the flop with Ace-high, I am still looking to reach a cheap showdown or see if the opponent checks back. Leading into the King doesn't fit my perceived range well, and Ace-high still maintains some marginal showdown value in a limped pot. I will check to see Claude's reaction; if he bets again on this texture, my hand is likely beat and I will fold, but checking remains the standard play here.

Turn board check from Flash suggests weakness. I have a solid pair of nines with potential straight outs. With my 3:1 chip lead and only 5 hands remaining, I should maximize value from my strong hand. A bet of 350 (~70% of pot) applies pressure while staying reasonable relative to the pot size. This should either win the pot immediately or build it when Flash calls with a worse hand.

I have Ace-high in a limped pot. The board is now Queen-King high with several middle cards (7, 9) making it quite connected. Claude bet on the flop and is now double-barreling on the turn after a King appeared. In a limped pot, Claude can easily have a King, Queen, or a straight draw that just got there or picked up equity. Since I have no pair and no real draw to improve, calling a second barrel with just Ace-high is too speculative, especially with the session nearing its end. I will fold and preserve my stack.

I am in the Small Blind with K9 offsuit. In heads-up play, K9o is a strong hand and worth a raise. Claude has been calling and raising quite frequently, but K9o is well ahead of his random range. I will raise to 300 to take control of the hand.

Folding 6d 4h from the BB against Flash's button raise. This is one of the weakest hands in poker and lacks the equity needed to call profitably. With a healthy stack (29,830) and only 3 hands remaining, preserving chips and waiting for a stronger hand is the correct strategic decision. Flash has shown aggressive tendencies, but even a wide range gives me insufficient equity to defend with 6-4 offsuit.

Premium hand in position - I have pocket aces (Ad, Ah) on the button/small blind with a significant stack advantage over Flash (29,780 vs 10,070). This is the strongest possible starting hand in Texas Hold'em. My objective is to build the pot and potentially get Flash all-in preflop where AA is a massive favorite. A raise to 500 (5x the big blind) is standard for this situation - aggressive enough to apply pressure and size the pot appropriately, but not so massive as to discourage Flash from playing back with premium hands. Given we're near the end of the tournament (hand 148 of 150), I should capitalize on this premium hand to maximize my edge and work toward a strong finish.

With only three hands remaining in the session, I am currently the shorter stack. Claude has opened to 500 from the small blind. Queen-Nine offsuit is a standard defending hand in a heads-up big blind versus small blind scenario. I have 100 big blinds, which gives me plenty of room to play post-flop. I will call the raise and see if I can connect with the board or find a profitable spot to outplay him later in the hand.

I have hit second pair (Queens) on a K-Q-3 rainbow board. While I am likely ahead of many of Claude's bluffs and smaller pairs, I am behind any King or a Queen with a better kicker. Checking to the pre-flop aggressor is the standard and most balanced play here to see how he proceeds and to keep the pot manageable.

Pocket aces on a K-Q-3 flop where opponent checked. Flash is short-stacked (9,670) and I have a commanding chip lead (29,330). With the strongest hand and 150 hands remaining, I must be aggressive. A 750 bet applies substantial pressure to his stack while extracting value from his checked weakness. This bet sizes appropriately to the pot while setting up potential all-in scenarios if he continues. Standard value bet with the premium holding.

On a board of K-Q-3 rainbow, I have second pair with a Nine kicker. Claude's pre-flop raise to 5x (500) and his continuation bet of nearly 43% of the pot (750) could represent a King, a better Queen (like AQ or QJ), or a straight draw like JT. However, it can also be a standard c-bet with many of his missed hands. Since there are only three hands left in the match and I need to protect my stack while seeking opportunities to gain ground, I will call here to see the turn. Folding second pair immediately on this texture would be too tight, but raising would likely only get called by hands that beat me.

On this turn card (7c), my hand strength remains unchanged as a second pair with a mediocre kicker. Given that Claude was the pre-flop aggressor and continued on the flop, he still has all the Kings (AK, KQ, KJ, KT) in his range, as well as some better Queens. I will check to him to see if he continues his aggression or takes a free card, which will help me better define his hand. Leading out into him doesn't accomplish much, as I'm unlikely to get a better hand to fold and will likely only be called or raised by hands that have me beat.

With pocket aces (AA) on a K-Q-3-7 board, I hold the strongest possible hand. Flash has shown clear weakness by checking both the flop and turn. He's also critically short-stacked with only 8,920 remaining while I have 28,580. 

This is hand 148 of 150, so maximizing value with premium holdings is critical. A turn bet of 1,400 (roughly 2.75x my previous flop bet) is standard aggression that:

1. Continues to apply pressure on his minimal stack
2. Represents strength consistent with my line (raised preflop, bet flop, continuing turn)
3. Extracts value from the strongest hand in poker
4. Sets up a river all-in scenario where my AA will be a dominant favorite

Flash's checks indicate he either missed entirely or has at best a mid-strength hand. There's no flush draw completed (no 4-flush on board), and the 7c doesn't create obvious straight possibilities. I need to capitalize on this strength and near-end-of-tournament position with aggressive value extraction.

On a board of K-Q-3-7, my second pair with a Nine kicker is a marginal holding. Claude opened for 5x pre-flop, c-bet 75% of the pot on the flop, and has now fired a second barrel of 1,400 into 2,500. This betting sequence from the chip leader is very strong and suggests either a King, a superior Queen (like AQ, QJ, or QT), or potentially a straight draw like JT. Given that I have only 8,920 chips remaining and the session is nearly over (Hand 148 of 150), calling here would likely commit me to an even larger, potentially stack-deciding bet on the river without much improvement. Since my Nine kicker is quite weak and Claude's range is heavily weighted toward value hands that currently have me beat, I will choose the disciplined route and fold to preserve my stack for the final two hands.

With T4o in the small blind, the hand is quite weak. Given the current game state with only two hands remaining and a significant stack deficit compared to Claude, there is little incentive to play a marginal hand out of position post-flop or risk chips on a low-equity steal attempt. Folding is the standard professional play here to conserve the remaining stack.

Final hand of the session (150/150). Playing on the button in heads-up against Flash who has a significantly shorter stack (8,770 vs my 31,080). I hold A9o which is a strong enough hand to raise with in this position. With my stack advantage and Flash's short stack, I should play aggressively to leverage my chip lead. A raise applies pressure and gives me the best chance to win chips in this final hand. Standard 3x raise (300 total) is appropriate for this situation.
